content.content:
1→# Sandra — Technical Implementation Roadmap
     2→
     3→Organization: EdLight Initiative  
     4→Project: Sandra  
     5→Document Type: Technical Implementation Roadmap  
     6→Status: Draft  
     7→Version: 1.0  
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→# 1. Purpose
    12→
    13→This roadmap defines the phased implementation plan for Sandra.
    14→
    15→It translates the architecture and data model into an execution path that is realistic, modular, and scalable.
    16→
    17→The roadmap is designed to help Sandra move from:
    18→
    19→- concept
    20→- to MVP
    21→- to multi-channel assistant
    22→- to secure user-aware platform
    23→- to true AI agent layer for the EdLight ecosystem
    24→
    25→This document focuses on:
    26→
    27→- technical build order
    28→- engineering priorities
    29→- milestones
    30→- deliverables
    31→- dependencies
    32→- what should be built now versus later
    33→
    34→---
    35→
    36→# 2. Guiding Principles
    37→
    38→## 2.1 Build the Core Once
    39→
    40→Sandra should not be built as separate bots for web, WhatsApp, Instagram, and voice.
    41→
    42→The correct approach is:
    43→
    44→- one core runtime
    45→- one tool layer
    46→- one data and permission model
    47→- multiple channel adapters
    48→
    49→---
    50→
    51→## 2.2 Ship a Focused MVP First
    52→
    53→The MVP should focus on:
    54→
    55→- web chat
    56→- public EdLight knowledge
    57→- repo indexing
    58→- clean architecture
    59→- strong internal abstractions
    60→
    61→The first version does not need to solve every integration.
    62→
    63→---
    64→
    65→## 2.3 Add Private Data Access Only After the Foundation Is Stable
    66→
    67→Sandra should first be good at:
    68→
    69→- answering public questions
    70→- understanding the ecosystem
    71→- retrieving documentation
    72→- maintaining sessions
    73→
    74→Only then should Sandra gain access to:
    75→
    76→- enrollments
    77→- certificates
    78→- application status
    79→- personalized workflows
    80→
    81→---
    82→
    83→## 2.4 Build for Extensibility
    84→
    85→Every major system should be modular:
    86→
    87→- channels
    88→- tools
    89→- connectors
    90→- retrieval
    91→- memory
    92→- indexing
    93→- permissions
    94→
    95→Sandra should be easy to extend when EdLight launches new products.
    96→
    97→---
    98→
    99→# 3. Recommended Delivery Strategy
   100→
   101→Sandra should be implemented in six major phases:
   102→
   103→1. Foundation and Developer Infrastructure  
   104→2. Core Sandra MVP  
   105→3. Knowledge and Repo Learning  
   106→4. Authenticated User Features  
   107→5. Messaging and Multi-Channel Expansion  
   108→6. Voice and Agentic Action Layer  
   109→
   110→Each phase should leave the system usable and incrementally more powerful.
   111→
   112→---
   113→
   114→# 4. Phase 0 — Planning and Repository Setup
   115→
   116→## Objective
   117→
   118→Prepare the Sandra repo, documentation, architecture, and engineering conventions before implementation begins.
   119→
   120→## Goals
   121→
   122→- establish repo structure
   123→- finalize technical direction
   124→- create implementation docs
   125→- define coding standards
   126→- prepare environment and secrets model
   127→
   128→## Deliverables
   129→
   130→- `README.md`
   131→- `docs/PRD.md`
   132→- `docs/architecture.md`
   133→- `docs/data-model.md`
   134→- `docs/roadmap.md`
   135→- `.env.example`
   136→- base repo structure
   137→- initial issue backlog
   138→
   139→## Suggested Folder Structure
   140→
   141→```text
   142→sandra/
   143→  apps/
   144→    web/
   145→    admin/
   146→  services/
   147→    agent-runtime/
   148→    gateway-api/
   149→    indexer/
   150→    workers/
   151→  packages/
   152→    auth/
   153→    channels/
   154→    tools/
   155→    connectors/
   156→    memory/
   157→    knowledge/
   158→    config/
   159→    shared-types/
   160→  docs/
   161→
   162→Exit Criteria
   163→	•	architecture docs exist
   164→	•	roadmap exists
   165→	•	folder structure is defined
   166→	•	core engineering choices are locked for MVP
   167→
   168→⸻
   169→
   170→5. Phase 1 — Foundation and Platform Setup
   171→
   172→Objective
   173→
   174→Build the core engineering foundation Sandra will rely on.
   175→
   176→Goals
   177→	•	set up the main app framework
   178→	•	set up auth foundation
   179→	•	set up database foundation
   180→	•	set up shared types and config
   181→	•	establish code quality and deployment basics
   182→
   183→Recommended Stack
   184→	•	Next.js
   185→	•	TypeScript
   186→	•	Tailwind CSS
   187→	•	Firebase Auth
   188→	•	Firestore
   189→	•	Postgres
   190→	•	Prisma
   191→	•	vector layer abstraction
   192→	•	Zod validation
   193→
   194→Workstreams
   195→
   196→5.1 Frontend and App Shell
   197→
   198→Build:
   199→	•	base Next.js app
   200→	•	routing
   201→	•	layout
   202→	•	Tailwind setup
   203→	•	base UI components
   204→
   205→5.2 Shared Config and Environment
   206→
   207→Build:
   208→	•	config loader
   209→	•	environment validation
   210→	•	runtime-safe config module
   211→	•	.env.example
   212→
   213→5.3 Database Layer
   214→
   215→Build:
   216→	•	Prisma setup
   217→	•	Postgres connection
   218→	•	initial schema
   219→	•	Firestore client setup
   220→	•	migration flow
   221→
   222→5.4 Auth Foundation
   223→
   224→Build:
   225→	•	Firebase Auth integration
   226→	•	token verification on backend
   227→	•	canonical user resolution flow
   228→	•	user session helpers
   229→
   230→5.5 Shared Types and Validation
   231→
   232→Build:
   233→	•	normalized request types
   234→	•	response envelope types
   235→	•	channel enums
   236→	•	language enums
   237→	•	permission enums
   238→	•	tool contract types
   239→	•	Zod schemas
   240→
   241→5.6 Developer Quality
   242→
   243→Set up:
   244→	•	linting
   245→	•	formatting
   246→	•	TypeScript strict mode
   247→	•	basic test setup
   248→	•	logging foundation
   249→
   250→Deliverables
   251→	•	working app shell
   252→	•	Firebase Auth connected
   253→	•	Postgres connected
   254→	•	Firestore connected
   255→	•	Prisma schema initialized
   256→	•	shared config and type packages
   257→	•	developer setup instructions
   258→
   259→Exit Criteria
   260→	•	app boots successfully
   261→	•	database connections work
   262→	•	auth verification works
   263→	•	shared config and schemas are in place
   264→
   265→⸻
   266→
   267→6. Phase 2 — Core Sandra MVP
   268→
   269→Objective
   270→
   271→Build the first working Sandra assistant for the web.
   272→
   273→Goals
   274→	•	create the Sandra runtime
   275→	•	create the first web chat experience
   276→	•	implement the tool layer foundation
   277→	•	implement session and conversation flow
   278→	•	enable public-question answering
   279→
   280→Scope
   281→
   282→This phase should only cover:
   283→	•	web chat
   284→	•	public knowledge
   285→	•	no private user data tools yet
   286→	•	no WhatsApp/Instagram/voice yet
   287→
   288→Workstreams
   289→
   290→6.1 Sandra Runtime
   291→
   292→Build:
   293→	•	request intake
   294→	•	language handling
   295→	•	session context loading
   296→	•	retrieval hook
   297→	•	tool orchestration hook
   298→	•	response generation flow
   299→
   300→6.2 Web Chat UI
   301→
   302→Build:
   303→	•	chat page
   304→	•	chat input
   305→	•	assistant response rendering
   306→	•	loading states
   307→	•	session persistence
   308→	•	empty state and branding-ready UI
   309→
   310→6.3 Message Persistence
   311→
   312→Build:
   313→	•	Firestore-backed session storage
   314→	•	Firestore chat messages
   315→	•	conversation history retrieval
   316→
   317→6.4 Gateway API Foundation
   318→
   319→Build:
   320→	•	POST /api/sandra/chat
   321→	•	GET /api/sandra/conversations/:sessionId
   322→	•	standard success/error envelope
   323→	•	input validation
   324→	•	request IDs
   325→	•	structured errors
   326→
   327→6.5 Initial Tools
   328→
   329→Implement:
   330→	•	searchKnowledgeBase
   331→	•	getEdLightInitiatives
   332→	•	lookupRepoInfo
   333→
   334→6.6 Language Support Foundation
   335→
   336→Implement:
   337→	•	Creole/French/English enums
   338→	•	response language selection
   339→	•	user language preference hook
   340→	•	system prompt helpers for language behavior
   341→
   342→Deliverables
   343→	•	functional Sandra web chat
   344→	•	messages stored in Firestore
   345→	•	first working runtime loop
   346→	•	first public tools working
   347→	•	API routes available
   348→
   349→Exit Criteria
   350→	•	a user can open Sandra on web
   351→	•	ask public questions about EdLight
   352→	•	receive answers through the core runtime
   353→	•	see message history in the same session
   354→
   355→⸻
   356→
   357→7. Phase 3 — Knowledge and Repo Learning
   358→
   359→Objective
   360→
   361→Teach Sandra the EdLight ecosystem through repo and documentation indexing.
   362→
   363→Goals
   364→	•	register EdLight repositories
   365→	•	fetch README/docs content
   366→	•	chunk and embed documents
   367→	•	store vectorized knowledge
   368→	•	retrieve relevant repo context at runtime
   369→
   370→Initial Repositories
   371→	•	EdLight Code
   372→	•	EdLight News
   373→	•	EdLight Initiative
   374→	•	EdLight Academy
   375→
   376→Workstreams
   377→
   378→7.1 Repo Registry
   379→
   380→Build:
   381→	•	repo_registry table
   382→	•	seed records for current repos
   383→	•	repo management service
   384→
   385→7.2 GitHub Connector
   386→
   387→Build:
   388→	•	repo metadata fetcher
   389→	•	README fetcher
   390→	•	docs path fetcher
   391→	•	generic content loader
   392→	•	GitHub auth support if needed
   393→
   394→7.3 Indexing Pipeline
   395→
   396→Build:
   397→	•	source extraction
   398→	•	text normalization
   399→	•	chunking
   400→	•	embedding generation
   401→	•	vector storage abstraction
   402→	•	indexed source metadata tracking
   403→
   404→7.4 Retrieval Service
   405→
   406→Build:
   407→	•	semantic search over indexed knowledge
   408→	•	ranking and filtering
   409→	•	initiative-aware retrieval
   410→	•	public/private visibility filtering
   411→
   412→7.5 Admin and Ops Visibility
   413→
   414→Build:
   415→	•	repo listing page
   416→	•	manual indexing trigger
   417→	•	indexing status view
   418→	•	indexed document count display
   419→
   420→Deliverables
   421→	•	current EdLight repos registered
   422→	•	README/docs indexed
   423→	•	Sandra can answer repo-aware questions
   424→	•	admin can see indexing state
   425→
   426→Exit Criteria
   427→	•	Sandra answers ecosystem questions using retrieved repo content
   428→	•	indexing can be triggered manually
   429→	•	new repos can be added to the registry without code rewrites
   430→
   431→⸻
   432→
   433→8. Phase 4 — Memory and Personalization Layer
   434→
   435→Objective
   436→
   437→Give Sandra a more structured memory model and persistent user-aware context.
   438→
   439→Goals
   440→	•	distinguish session memory from long-term memory
   441→	•	persist user preferences
   442→	•	improve conversation continuity
   443→	•	prepare for authenticated user features
   444→
   445→Workstreams
   446→
   447→8.1 Session Memory
   448→
   449→Build:
   450→	•	short-term conversation summarization strategy
   451→	•	recent context retrieval
   452→	•	context window management
   453→
   454→8.2 User Memory Abstraction
   455→
   456→Build:
   457→	•	user preference storage
   458→	•	preferred language
   459→	•	initiative interests
   460→	•	learning interests
   461→	•	reusable personalization hooks
   462→
   463→8.3 Memory Services
   464→
   465→Build:
   466→	•	memory save service
   467→	•	memory retrieval service
   468→	•	memory scoping rules
   469→	•	user-linked memory resolution
   470→
   471→8.4 Runtime Integration
   472→
   473→Update Sandra Runtime to:
   474→	•	load memory by user ID or session ID
   475→	•	use memory carefully in prompts
   476→	•	write back memory-worthy facts under explicit rules
   477→
   478→Deliverables
   479→	•	memory abstractions
   480→	•	session context persistence improvements
   481→	•	early personalization support
   482→
   483→Exit Criteria
   484→	•	Sandra remembers language preference
   485→	•	Sandra can maintain better conversation continuity
   486→	•	memory interfaces are stable enough for future expansion
   487→
   488→⸻
   489→
   490→9. Phase 5 — Authenticated User Features
   491→
   492→Objective
   493→
   494→Allow Sandra to securely answer user-specific questions.
   495→
   496→Goals
   497→	•	connect authenticated users to canonical records
   498→	•	add private-data tools
   499→	•	enforce permission checks
   500→	•	log sensitive access
   501→
   502→Workstreams
   503→
   504→9.1 Canonical User Mapping
   505→
   506→Build:
   507→	•	Firebase UID to Postgres user mapping
   508→	•	user provisioning flow
   509→	•	user identity sync logic
   510→
   511→9.2 Permission Engine
   512→
   513→Build:
   514→	•	role resolution
   515→	•	scope resolution
   516→	•	ownership checks
   517→	•	tool execution authorization middleware
   518→
   519→9.3 Private Data Models
   520→
   521→Implement initial Postgres-backed domains for:
   522→	•	users
   523→	•	roles
   524→	•	permissions
   525→	•	enrollments
   526→	•	certificates
   527→	•	program applications
   528→	•	consents
   529→
   530→9.4 Private User Tools
   531→
   532→Implement:
   533→	•	getUserCertificates
   534→	•	getUserEnrollments
   535→	•	getApplicationStatus
   536→	•	getUserProfileSummary
   537→
   538→9.5 Audit Layer
   539→
   540→Build:
   541→	•	tool_executions
   542→	•	audit_logs
   543→	•	permission denial logs
   544→	•	sensitive action event logging
   545→
   546→Deliverables
   547→	•	signed-in user resolution
   548→	•	first secure private tools
   549→	•	permissions enforced for every private tool
   550→	•	audit logs working
   551→
   552→Exit Criteria
   553→	•	a signed-in user can ask Sandra for personal data
   554→	•	Sandra only returns authorized records
   555→	•	every sensitive action is logged
   556→
   557→⸻
   558→
   559→10. Phase 6 — Sandra Gateway Expansion
   560→
   561→Objective
   562→
   563→Expand the internal tool and connector ecosystem.
   564→
   565→Goals
   566→	•	make Sandra useful beyond answering questions
   567→	•	connect more EdLight systems
   568→	•	expose action-oriented tools
   569→	•	normalize access through the gateway
   570→
   571→Workstreams
   572→
   573→10.1 Gateway Hardening
   574→
   575→Improve:
   576→	•	tool execution lifecycle
   577→	•	retries where appropriate
   578→	•	timeout handling
   579→	•	structured error mapping
   580→	•	request correlation
   581→
   582→10.2 Connector Framework
   583→
   584→Implement connector standards for:
   585→	•	EdLight Code
   586→	•	EdLight Academy
   587→	•	EdLight News
   588→	•	EdLight Initiative
   589→	•	GitHub
   590→
   591→10.3 Additional Tools
   592→
   593→Examples:
   594→	•	getCourseCatalog
   595→	•	getLatestNews
   596→	•	getProgramDeadlines
   597→	•	searchScholarships
   598→	•	getInitiativeLinks
   599→
   600→10.4 Internal Admin Tools
   601→
   602→Examples:
   603→	•	triggerRepoIndexing
   604→	•	getIndexingStatus
   605→	•	listConnectedSystems
   606→	•	viewSystemHealth
   607→
   608→Deliverables
   609→	•	stronger tool registry
   610→	•	stronger connector architecture
   611→	•	wider ecosystem support
   612→	•	more operational visibility
   613→
   614→Exit Criteria
   615→	•	Sandra can act as a serious ecosystem interface
   616→	•	new tools can be added without changing the runtime architecture
   617→
   618→⸻
   619→
   620→11. Phase 7 — WhatsApp Integration
   621→
   622→Objective
   623→
   624→Launch Sandra on WhatsApp using the same core runtime.
   625→
   626→Goals
   627→	•	add WhatsApp as a channel adapter
   628→	•	support inbound and outbound messaging
   629→	•	support user linking and session continuity
   630→	•	keep web and WhatsApp behavior consistent
   631→
   632→Workstreams
   633→
   634→11.1 WhatsApp Adapter
   635→
   636→Build:
   637→	•	webhook handler
   638→	•	inbound message normalization
   639→	•	outbound message formatting
   640→	•	delivery/error tracking
   641→
   642→11.2 Session Linking
   643→
   644→Build:
   645→	•	external WhatsApp identity mapping
   646→	•	session continuity rules
   647→	•	phone-based user association logic
   648→
   649→11.3 Tool Compatibility
   650→
   651→Ensure the same runtime can serve WhatsApp requests with:
   652→	•	public content tools
   653→	•	authenticated tools where linked identity exists
   654→
   655→Deliverables
   656→	•	working WhatsApp Sandra
   657→	•	shared runtime with web
   658→	•	linked conversation flow
   659→
   660→Exit Criteria
   661→	•	a user can message Sandra on WhatsApp
   662→	•	Sandra responds using the same core brain as web
   663→	•	message history and identity linkage work as designed
   664→
   665→⸻
   666→
   667→12. Phase 8 — Email and Instagram Integration
   668→
   669→Objective
   670→
   671→Expand Sandra into additional communication channels.
   672→
   673→Goals
   674→	•	add email intake and response support
   675→	•	add Instagram DM support
   676→	•	normalize both into the same runtime
   677→
   678→Workstreams
   679→
   680→12.1 Email Adapter
   681→
   682→Build:
   683→	•	inbound email parser
   684→	•	outbound email sender abstraction
   685→	•	message threading logic
   686→	•	permission-aware email operations
   687→
   688→12.2 Instagram Adapter
   689→
   690→Build:
   691→	•	inbound DM handler
   692→	•	outbound response formatting
   693→	•	session state rules
   694→
   695→12.3 Channel-Specific Formatting
   696→
   697→Implement response formatting differences for:
   698→	•	short chat channels
   699→	•	email-style longer responses
   700→	•	platform constraints
   701→
   702→Deliverables
   703→	•	Sandra on email
   704→	•	Sandra on Instagram
   705→	•	channel adapters integrated into the shared runtime
   706→
   707→Exit Criteria
   708→	•	Sandra supports at least 4 channels with one shared core architecture
   709→
   710→⸻
   711→
   712→13. Phase 9 — Voice Layer
   713→
   714→Objective
   715→
   716→Enable Sandra to communicate by voice.
   717→
   718→Goals
   719→	•	speech-to-text
   720→	•	text-to-speech
   721→	•	voice session handling
   722→	•	voice-ready response shaping
   723→
   724→Workstreams
   725→
   726→13.1 Voice Input Pipeline
   727→
   728→Build:
   729→	•	audio upload or live stream handling
   730→	•	transcription service integration
   731→	•	transcription normalization
   732→
   733→13.2 Voice Output Pipeline
   734→
   735→Build:
   736→	•	TTS service integration
   737→	•	multilingual voice output support
   738→	•	audio delivery format rules
   739→
   740→13.3 Voice Conversation Runtime Adaptation
   741→
   742→Update Sandra to:
   743→	•	respond more naturally for spoken interactions
   744→	•	manage shorter, clearer answers when in voice mode
   745→	•	handle interruptions or reprompts later
   746→
   747→Deliverables
   748→	•	voice input support
   749→	•	spoken response generation
   750→	•	voice-aware runtime behavior
   751→
   752→Exit Criteria
   753→	•	a user can talk to Sandra and receive spoken replies
   754→
   755→⸻
   756→
   757→14. Phase 10 — Agentic Action Layer
   758→
   759→Objective
   760→
   761→Move Sandra from assistant to action-capable agent.
   762→
   763→Goals
   764→	•	support multi-step task execution
   765→	•	perform safe actions across EdLight systems
   766→	•	add workflows and automations
   767→	•	maintain strict control and auditability
   768→
   769→Workstreams
   770→
   771→14.1 Action Tooling
   772→
   773→Build tools such as:
   774→	•	createLead
   775→	•	queueReminder
   776→	•	draftEmail
   777→	•	sendMessage
   778→	•	submitInterestForm
   779→	•	recommendCourses
   780→
   781→14.2 Workflow Engine
   782→
   783→Build support for:
   784→	•	multi-step tool sequences
   785→	•	stateful workflows
   786→	•	retries and checkpoints
   787→	•	controlled autonomy boundaries
   788→
   789→14.3 Human-in-the-Loop Controls
   790→
   791→Implement:
   792→	•	approval requirements for sensitive actions
   793→	•	admin approval queues where needed
   794→	•	safe-action boundaries
   795→
   796→14.4 Operational Guardrails
   797→
   798→Add:
   799→	•	stronger policy rules
   800→	•	action rate limiting
   801→	•	anomaly logging
   802→	•	action review tools
   803→
   804→Deliverables
   805→	•	first real agent workflows
   806→	•	safe action-taking support
   807→	•	human review path for risky operations
   808→
   809→Exit Criteria
   810→	•	Sandra can complete bounded multi-step tasks safely
   811→	•	action workflows are auditable and permission-aware
   812→
   813→⸻
   814→
   815→15. Phase 11 — Advanced Intelligence and Optimization
   816→
   817→Objective
   818→
   819→Improve Sandra’s quality, speed, and usefulness over time.
   820→
   821→Goals
   822→	•	improve retrieval quality
   823→	•	improve personalization
   824→	•	improve response quality
   825→	•	optimize cost and latency
   826→	•	improve analytics and learning loops
   827→

   840→

   875→
   876→16. Recommended Milestone Sequence
   877→
   878→Milestone 1 — Repo and foundation
   879→
   880→Complete:
   881→	•	docs

   887→Milestone 2 — Sandra web MVP
   888→
   889→Complete:
   890→	•	runtime

   894→
   895→Milestone 3 — Repo learning
   896→
   897→Complete:
   898→	•	repo registry
   899→	•	GitHub ingestion
   900→	•	indexing

   902→
   903→Milestone 4 — User-aware Sandra
   904→
   905→Complete:
   906→	•	canonical users
   907→	•	permissions
   908→	•	certificates/enrollments/application tools
   909→
   910→Milestone 5 — Multi-channel Sandra
   911→
   912→Complete:
   913→	•	WhatsApp
   914→	•	email
   915→	•	Instagram
   916→
   917→Milestone 6 — Voice and agent actions
   918→
   919→Complete:
   920→	•	voice
   921→	•	controlled actions
   922→	•	workflow support
   923→
   924→⸻
   925→
   926→17. Suggested Priority Order for Engineering
   927→
   928→If engineering time is limited, build in this order:
   929→	1.	architecture and docs
   930→	2.	shared types and config
   931→	3.	Postgres + Firestore + auth foundation
   932→	4.	web chat UI
   933→	5.	Sandra runtime
   934→	6.	public tools

   937→	9.	canonical users and permissions
   938→	10.	private tools
   939→	11.	WhatsApp
   940→	12.	email and Instagram
   941→	13.	voice
   942→	14.	action workflows

   944→This order keeps Sandra useful early while protecting the long-term architecture.
   945→
   946→⸻
   947→
   948→18. Suggested MVP Definition
   949→
   950→Sandra MVP should mean:
   951→	•	web-based assistant
   952→	•	multilingual support foundation
   953→	•	answers public questions about EdLight
   954→	•	understands indexed repo documentation
   955→	•	stores chat sessions
   956→	•	has clean runtime/tool/retrieval architecture
   957→	•	includes admin repo indexing controls
   958→
   959→Sandra MVP should not require:
   960→	•	WhatsApp
   961→	•	Instagram
   962→	•	email
   963→	•	voice
   964→	•	advanced personalization
   965→	•	autonomous workflows
   966→

   968→

   971→19. Suggested Post-MVP Definition
   972→
   973→Sandra post-MVP should include:
   974→	•	authenticated user support
   975→	•	certificates and enrollment retrieval
   976→	•	permissions and audit logs
   977→	•	stronger memory model
   978→	•	WhatsApp support
   979→	•	improved admin operations
   980→
   981→⸻
   982→
   983→20. Suggested Long-Term Definition
   984→
   985→Sandra long-term platform should include:
   986→	•	all major EdLight channels
   987→	•	repo auto-learning
   988→	•	user-aware guidance
   989→	•	voice support
   990→	•	tool-driven actions
   991→	•	cross-initiative orchestration
   992→	•	internal operational tooling
   993→	•	strong observability and compliance controls
   994→
   995→At that point Sandra becomes the AI operating layer of the EdLight ecosystem.
   996→
   997→⸻
   998→
   999→21. Risks by Phase
  1000→
  1001→Early Risks
  1002→	•	overbuilding before MVP
  1003→	•	muddy architecture
  1004→	•	too many channel integrations too soon
  1005→
  1006→Mid-Phase Risks
  1007→	•	poor permission design
  1008→	•	weak auditability
  1009→	•	inconsistent connector contracts
  1010→
  1011→Late Risks
  1012→	•	unsafe autonomous actions
  1013→	•	scaling complexity
  1014→	•	fragmented system behavior across channels
  1015→
  1016→⸻
  1017→
  1018→22. Recommendation
  1019→
  1020→The best way to build Sandra is:
  1021→	•	web first
  1022→	•	public knowledge first
  1023→	•	repo learning second
  1024→	•	authenticated private tools third
  1025→	•	messaging channels fourth
  1026→	•	voice fifth
  1027→	•	autonomous workflows last
  1028→
  1029→This sequence is the best balance between:
  1030→	•	speed
  1031→	•	safety
  1032→	•	maintainability
  1033→	•	product value
  1034→
  1035→⸻
  1036→
  1037→23. Final Summary
  1038→
  1039→Sandra should be built as a modular AI platform, not as a one-off chatbot.
  1040→
  1041→The implementation roadmap should prioritize:
  1042→	•	one shared runtime
  1043→	•	one shared tool layer
  1044→	•	one consistent permission model
  1045→	•	one consistent knowledge pipeline
  1046→	•	multiple channel adapters over time
  1047→
  1048→If implemented in this order, Sandra can grow from an MVP assistant into a true multi-system AI agent serving the full EdLight ecosystem.
  1049→

# Sandra — Technical Implementation Roadmap

Organization: EdLight Initiative  
Project: Sandra  
Document Type: Technical Implementation Roadmap  
Status: Draft  
Version: 1.0  

---

# 1. Purpose

This roadmap defines the phased implementation plan for Sandra.

It translates the architecture and data model into an execution path that is realistic, modular, and scalable.

The roadmap is designed to help Sandra move from:

- concept
- to MVP
- to multi-channel assistant
- to secure user-aware platform
- to true AI agent layer for the EdLight ecosystem

This document focuses on:

- technical build order
- engineering priorities
- milestones
- deliverables
- dependencies
- what should be built now versus later

---

# 2. Guiding Principles

## 2.1 Build the Core Once

Sandra should not be built as separate bots for web, WhatsApp, Instagram, and voice.

The correct approach is:

- one core runtime
- one tool layer
- one data and permission model
- multiple channel adapters

---

## 2.2 Ship a Focused MVP First

The MVP should focus on:

- web chat
- public EdLight knowledge
- repo indexing
- clean architecture
- strong internal abstractions

The first version does not need to solve every integration.

---

## 2.3 Add Private Data Access Only After the Foundation Is Stable

Sandra should first be good at:

- answering public questions
- understanding the ecosystem
- retrieving documentation
- maintaining sessions

Only then should Sandra gain access to:

- enrollments
- certificates
- application status
- personalized workflows

---

## 2.4 Build for Extensibility

Every major system should be modular:

- channels
- tools
- connectors
- retrieval
- memory
- indexing
- permissions

Sandra should be easy to extend when EdLight launches new products.

---

# 3. Recommended Delivery Strategy

Sandra should be implemented in six major phases:

1. Foundation and Developer Infrastructure  
2. Core Sandra MVP  
3. Knowledge and Repo Learning  
4. Authenticated User Features  
5. Messaging and Multi-Channel Expansion  
6. Voice and Agentic Action Layer  

Each phase should leave the system usable and incrementally more powerful.

---

# 4. Phase 0 — Planning and Repository Setup

## Objective

Prepare the Sandra repo, documentation, architecture, and engineering conventions before implementation begins.

## Goals

- establish repo structure
- finalize technical direction
- create implementation docs
- define coding standards
- prepare environment and secrets model

## Deliverables

- `README.md`
- `docs/PRD.md`
- `docs/architecture.md`
- `docs/data-model.md`
- `docs/roadmap.md`
- `.env.example`
- base repo structure
- initial issue backlog

## Suggested Folder Structure

```text
sandra/
  apps/
    web/
    admin/
  services/
    agent-runtime/
    gateway-api/
    indexer/
    workers/
  packages/
    auth/
    channels/
    tools/
    connectors/
    memory/
    knowledge/
    config/
    shared-types/
  docs/

Exit Criteria
	•	architecture docs exist
	•	roadmap exists
	•	folder structure is defined
	•	core engineering choices are locked for MVP

⸻

5. Phase 1 — Foundation and Platform Setup

Objective

Build the core engineering foundation Sandra will rely on.

Goals
	•	set up the main app framework
	•	set up auth foundation
	•	set up database foundation
	•	set up shared types and config
	•	establish code quality and deployment basics

Recommended Stack
	•	Next.js
	•	TypeScript
	•	Tailwind CSS
	•	Firebase Auth
	•	Firestore
	•	Postgres
	•	Prisma
	•	vector layer abstraction
	•	Zod validation

Workstreams

5.1 Frontend and App Shell

Build:
	•	base Next.js app
	•	routing
	•	layout
	•	Tailwind setup
	•	base UI components

5.2 Shared Config and Environment

Build:
	•	config loader
	•	environment validation
	•	runtime-safe config module
	•	.env.example

5.3 Database Layer

Build:
	•	Prisma setup
	•	Postgres connection
	•	initial schema
	•	Firestore client setup
	•	migration flow

5.4 Auth Foundation

Build:
	•	Firebase Auth integration
	•	token verification on backend
	•	canonical user resolution flow
	•	user session helpers

5.5 Shared Types and Validation

Build:
	•	normalized request types
	•	response envelope types
	•	channel enums
	•	language enums
	•	permission enums
	•	tool contract types
	•	Zod schemas

5.6 Developer Quality

Set up:
	•	linting
	•	formatting
	•	TypeScript strict mode
	•	basic test setup
	•	logging foundation

Deliverables
	•	working app shell
	•	Firebase Auth connected
	•	Postgres connected
	•	Firestore connected
	•	Prisma schema initialized
	•	shared config and type packages
	•	developer setup instructions

Exit Criteria
	•	app boots successfully
	•	database connections work
	•	auth verification works
	•	shared config and schemas are in place

⸻

6. Phase 2 — Core Sandra MVP

Objective

Build the first working Sandra assistant for the web.

Goals
	•	create the Sandra runtime
	•	create the first web chat experience
	•	implement the tool layer foundation
	•	implement session and conversation flow
	•	enable public-question answering

Scope

This phase should only cover:
	•	web chat
	•	public knowledge
	•	no private user data tools yet
	•	no WhatsApp/Instagram/voice yet

Workstreams

6.1 Sandra Runtime

Build:
	•	request intake
	•	language handling
	•	session context loading
	•	retrieval hook
	•	tool orchestration hook
	•	response generation flow

6.2 Web Chat UI

Build:
	•	chat page
	•	chat input
	•	assistant response rendering
	•	loading states
	•	session persistence
	•	empty state and branding-ready UI

6.3 Message Persistence

Build:
	•	Firestore-backed session storage
	•	Firestore chat messages
	•	conversation history retrieval

6.4 Gateway API Foundation

Build:
	•	POST /api/sandra/chat
	•	GET /api/sandra/conversations/:sessionId
	•	standard success/error envelope
	•	input validation
	•	request IDs
	•	structured errors

6.5 Initial Tools

Implement:
	•	searchKnowledgeBase
	•	getEdLightInitiatives
	•	lookupRepoInfo

6.6 Language Support Foundation

Implement:
	•	Creole/French/English enums
	•	response language selection
	•	user language preference hook
	•	system prompt helpers for language behavior

Deliverables
	•	functional Sandra web chat
	•	messages stored in Firestore
	•	first working runtime loop
	•	first public tools working
	•	API routes available

Exit Criteria
	•	a user can open Sandra on web
	•	ask public questions about EdLight
	•	receive answers through the core runtime
	•	see message history in the same session

⸻

7. Phase 3 — Knowledge and Repo Learning

Objective

Teach Sandra the EdLight ecosystem through repo and documentation indexing.

Goals
	•	register EdLight repositories
	•	fetch README/docs content
	•	chunk and embed documents
	•	store vectorized knowledge
	•	retrieve relevant repo context at runtime

Initial Repositories
	•	EdLight Code
	•	EdLight News
	•	EdLight Initiative
	•	EdLight Academy

Workstreams

7.1 Repo Registry

Build:
	•	repo_registry table
	•	seed records for current repos
	•	repo management service

7.2 GitHub Connector

Build:
	•	repo metadata fetcher
	•	README fetcher
	•	docs path fetcher
	•	generic content loader
	•	GitHub auth support if needed

7.3 Indexing Pipeline

Build:
	•	source extraction
	•	text normalization
	•	chunking
	•	embedding generation
	•	vector storage abstraction
	•	indexed source metadata tracking

7.4 Retrieval Service

Build:
	•	semantic search over indexed knowledge
	•	ranking and filtering
	•	initiative-aware retrieval
	•	public/private visibility filtering

7.5 Admin and Ops Visibility

Build:
	•	repo listing page
	•	manual indexing trigger
	•	indexing status view
	•	indexed document count display

Deliverables
	•	current EdLight repos registered
	•	README/docs indexed
	•	Sandra can answer repo-aware questions
	•	admin can see indexing state

Exit Criteria
	•	Sandra answers ecosystem questions using retrieved repo content
	•	indexing can be triggered manually
	•	new repos can be added to the registry without code rewrites

⸻

8. Phase 4 — Memory and Personalization Layer

Objective

Give Sandra a more structured memory model and persistent user-aware context.

Goals
	•	distinguish session memory from long-term memory
	•	persist user preferences
	•	improve conversation continuity
	•	prepare for authenticated user features

Workstreams

8.1 Session Memory

Build:
	•	short-term conversation summarization strategy
	•	recent context retrieval
	•	context window management

8.2 User Memory Abstraction

Build:
	•	user preference storage
	•	preferred language
	•	initiative interests
	•	learning interests
	•	reusable personalization hooks

8.3 Memory Services

Build:
	•	memory save service
	•	memory retrieval service
	•	memory scoping rules
	•	user-linked memory resolution

8.4 Runtime Integration

Update Sandra Runtime to:
	•	load memory by user ID or session ID
	•	use memory carefully in prompts
	•	write back memory-worthy facts under explicit rules

Deliverables
	•	memory abstractions
	•	session context persistence improvements
	•	early personalization support

Exit Criteria
	•	Sandra remembers language preference
	•	Sandra can maintain better conversation continuity
	•	memory interfaces are stable enough for future expansion

⸻

9. Phase 5 — Authenticated User Features

Objective

Allow Sandra to securely answer user-specific questions.

Goals
	•	connect authenticated users to canonical records
	•	add private-data tools
	•	enforce permission checks
	•	log sensitive access

Workstreams

9.1 Canonical User Mapping

Build:
	•	Firebase UID to Postgres user mapping
	•	user provisioning flow
	•	user identity sync logic

9.2 Permission Engine

Build:
	•	role resolution
	•	scope resolution
	•	ownership checks
	•	tool execution authorization middleware

9.3 Private Data Models

Implement initial Postgres-backed domains for:
	•	users
	•	roles
	•	permissions
	•	enrollments
	•	certificates
	•	program applications
	•	consents

9.4 Private User Tools

Implement:
	•	getUserCertificates
	•	getUserEnrollments
	•	getApplicationStatus
	•	getUserProfileSummary

9.5 Audit Layer

Build:
	•	tool_executions
	•	audit_logs
	•	permission denial logs
	•	sensitive action event logging

Deliverables
	•	signed-in user resolution
	•	first secure private tools
	•	permissions enforced for every private tool
	•	audit logs working

Exit Criteria
	•	a signed-in user can ask Sandra for personal data
	•	Sandra only returns authorized records
	•	every sensitive action is logged

⸻

10. Phase 6 — Sandra Gateway Expansion

Objective

Expand the internal tool and connector ecosystem.

Goals
	•	make Sandra useful beyond answering questions
	•	connect more EdLight systems
	•	expose action-oriented tools
	•	normalize access through the gateway

Workstreams

10.1 Gateway Hardening

Improve:
	•	tool execution lifecycle
	•	retries where appropriate
	•	timeout handling
	•	structured error mapping
	•	request correlation

10.2 Connector Framework

Implement connector standards for:
	•	EdLight Code
	•	EdLight Academy
	•	EdLight News
	•	EdLight Initiative
	•	GitHub

10.3 Additional Tools

Examples:
	•	getCourseCatalog
	•	getLatestNews
	•	getProgramDeadlines
	•	searchScholarships
	•	getInitiativeLinks

10.4 Internal Admin Tools

Examples:
	•	triggerRepoIndexing
	•	getIndexingStatus
	•	listConnectedSystems
	•	viewSystemHealth

Deliverables
	•	stronger tool registry
	•	stronger connector architecture
	•	wider ecosystem support
	•	more operational visibility

Exit Criteria
	•	Sandra can act as a serious ecosystem interface
	•	new tools can be added without changing the runtime architecture

⸻

11. Phase 7 — WhatsApp Integration

Objective

Launch Sandra on WhatsApp using the same core runtime.

Goals
	•	add WhatsApp as a channel adapter
	•	support inbound and outbound messaging
	•	support user linking and session continuity
	•	keep web and WhatsApp behavior consistent

Workstreams

11.1 WhatsApp Adapter

Build:
	•	webhook handler
	•	inbound message normalization
	•	outbound message formatting
	•	delivery/error tracking

11.2 Session Linking

Build:
	•	external WhatsApp identity mapping
	•	session continuity rules
	•	phone-based user association logic

11.3 Tool Compatibility

Ensure the same runtime can serve WhatsApp requests with:
	•	public content tools
	•	authenticated tools where linked identity exists

Deliverables
	•	working WhatsApp Sandra
	•	shared runtime with web
	•	linked conversation flow

Exit Criteria
	•	a user can message Sandra on WhatsApp
	•	Sandra responds using the same core brain as web
	•	message history and identity linkage work as designed

⸻

12. Phase 8 — Email and Instagram Integration

Objective

Expand Sandra into additional communication channels.

Goals
	•	add email intake and response support
	•	add Instagram DM support
	•	normalize both into the same runtime

Workstreams

12.1 Email Adapter

Build:
	•	inbound email parser
	•	outbound email sender abstraction
	•	message threading logic
	•	permission-aware email operations

12.2 Instagram Adapter

Build:
	•	inbound DM handler
	•	outbound response formatting
	•	session state rules

12.3 Channel-Specific Formatting

Implement response formatting differences for:
	•	short chat channels
	•	email-style longer responses
	•	platform constraints

Deliverables
	•	Sandra on email
	•	Sandra on Instagram
	•	channel adapters integrated into the shared runtime

Exit Criteria
	•	Sandra supports at least 4 channels with one shared core architecture

⸻

13. Phase 9 — Voice Layer

Objective

Enable Sandra to communicate by voice.

Goals
	•	speech-to-text
	•	text-to-speech
	•	voice session handling
	•	voice-ready response shaping

Workstreams

13.1 Voice Input Pipeline

Build:
	•	audio upload or live stream handling
	•	transcription service integration
	•	transcription normalization

13.2 Voice Output Pipeline

Build:
	•	TTS service integration
	•	multilingual voice output support
	•	audio delivery format rules

13.3 Voice Conversation Runtime Adaptation

Update Sandra to:
	•	respond more naturally for spoken interactions
	•	manage shorter, clearer answers when in voice mode
	•	handle interruptions or reprompts later

Deliverables
	•	voice input support
	•	spoken response generation
	•	voice-aware runtime behavior

Exit Criteria
	•	a user can talk to Sandra and receive spoken replies

⸻

14. Phase 10 — Agentic Action Layer

Objective

Move Sandra from assistant to action-capable agent.

Goals
	•	support multi-step task execution
	•	perform safe actions across EdLight systems
	•	add workflows and automations
	•	maintain strict control and auditability

Workstreams

14.1 Action Tooling

Build tools such as:
	•	createLead
	•	queueReminder
	•	draftEmail
	•	sendMessage
	•	submitInterestForm
	•	recommendCourses

14.2 Workflow Engine

Build support for:
	•	multi-step tool sequences
	•	stateful workflows
	•	retries and checkpoints
	•	controlled autonomy boundaries

14.3 Human-in-the-Loop Controls

Implement:
	•	approval requirements for sensitive actions
	•	admin approval queues where needed
	•	safe-action boundaries

14.4 Operational Guardrails

Add:
	•	stronger policy rules
	•	action rate limiting
	•	anomaly logging
	•	action review tools

Deliverables
	•	first real agent workflows
	•	safe action-taking support
	•	human review path for risky operations

Exit Criteria
	•	Sandra can complete bounded multi-step tasks safely
	•	action workflows are auditable and permission-aware

⸻

15. Phase 11 — Advanced Intelligence and Optimization

Objective

Improve Sandra’s quality, speed, and usefulness over time.

Goals
	•	improve retrieval quality
	•	improve personalization
	•	improve response quality
	•	optimize cost and latency
	•	improve analytics and learning loops

Workstreams

15.1 Analytics

Track:
	•	question categories
	•	tool usage
	•	retrieval success
	•	response quality
	•	drop-off points
	•	channel usage
	•	language usage

15.2 Prompt and Tool Optimization

Improve:
	•	system prompts
	•	retrieval prompts
	•	tool selection logic
	•	fallback behavior

15.3 Recommendation Layer

Add:
	•	course recommendations
	•	initiative recommendations
	•	scholarship relevance scoring
	•	proactive educational guidance

15.4 Cost and Performance Optimization

Improve:
	•	caching
	•	retrieval efficiency
	•	model routing
	•	background processing strategy

Deliverables
	•	analytics dashboards
	•	improved response quality
	•	early recommendation features
	•	better system efficiency

Exit Criteria
	•	Sandra is measurably improving through operational feedback

⸻

16. Recommended Milestone Sequence

Milestone 1 — Repo and foundation

Complete:
	•	docs
	•	base repo
	•	config
	•	database setup
	•	auth setup

Milestone 2 — Sandra web MVP

Complete:
	•	runtime
	•	web chat
	•	Firestore messages
	•	public knowledge answers

Milestone 3 — Repo learning

Complete:
	•	repo registry
	•	GitHub ingestion
	•	indexing
	•	retrieval-backed answers

Milestone 4 — User-aware Sandra

Complete:
	•	canonical users
	•	permissions
	•	certificates/enrollments/application tools

Milestone 5 — Multi-channel Sandra

Complete:
	•	WhatsApp
	•	email
	•	Instagram

Milestone 6 — Voice and agent actions

Complete:
	•	voice
	•	controlled actions
	•	workflow support

⸻

17. Suggested Priority Order for Engineering

If engineering time is limited, build in this order:
	1.	architecture and docs
	2.	shared types and config
	3.	Postgres + Firestore + auth foundation
	4.	web chat UI
	5.	Sandra runtime
	6.	public tools
	7.	repo indexing and retrieval
	8.	admin indexing controls
	9.	canonical users and permissions
	10.	private tools
	11.	WhatsApp
	12.	email and Instagram
	13.	voice
	14.	action workflows

This order keeps Sandra useful early while protecting the long-term architecture.

⸻

18. Suggested MVP Definition

Sandra MVP should mean:
	•	web-based assistant
	•	multilingual support foundation
	•	answers public questions about EdLight
	•	understands indexed repo documentation
	•	stores chat sessions
	•	has clean runtime/tool/retrieval architecture
	•	includes admin repo indexing controls

Sandra MVP should not require:
	•	WhatsApp
	•	Instagram
	•	email
	•	voice
	•	advanced personalization
	•	autonomous workflows

Those come later.

⸻

19. Suggested Post-MVP Definition

Sandra post-MVP should include:
	•	authenticated user support
	•	certificates and enrollment retrieval
	•	permissions and audit logs
	•	stronger memory model
	•	WhatsApp support
	•	improved admin operations

⸻

20. Suggested Long-Term Definition

Sandra long-term platform should include:
	•	all major EdLight channels
	•	repo auto-learning
	•	user-aware guidance
	•	voice support
	•	tool-driven actions
	•	cross-initiative orchestration
	•	internal operational tooling
	•	strong observability and compliance controls

At that point Sandra becomes the AI operating layer of the EdLight ecosystem.

⸻

21. Risks by Phase

Early Risks
	•	overbuilding before MVP
	•	muddy architecture
	•	too many channel integrations too soon

Mid-Phase Risks
	•	poor permission design
	•	weak auditability
	•	inconsistent connector contracts

Late Risks
	•	unsafe autonomous actions
	•	scaling complexity
	•	fragmented system behavior across channels

⸻

22. Recommendation

The best way to build Sandra is:
	•	web first
	•	public knowledge first
	•	repo learning second
	•	authenticated private tools third
	•	messaging channels fourth
	•	voice fifth
	•	autonomous workflows last

This sequence is the best balance between:
	•	speed
	•	safety
	•	maintainability
	•	product value

⸻

23. Final Summary

Sandra should be built as a modular AI platform, not as a one-off chatbot.

The implementation roadmap should prioritize:
	•	one shared runtime
	•	one shared tool layer
	•	one consistent permission model
	•	one consistent knowledge pipeline
	•	multiple channel adapters over time

If implemented in this order, Sandra can grow from an MVP assistant into a true multi-system AI agent serving the full EdLight ecosystem.

⸻

# Sandra — Implementation PRD (Version 1)

Organization: EdLight Initiative  
Project: Sandra  
Document Type: Implementation Product Requirements Document  
Status: Active  
Version: 1.0  

---

# 1. Executive Summary

Sandra is the AI assistant for the EdLight ecosystem.

Sandra will act as the central conversational interface connecting EdLight platforms such as:

- EdLight Code
- EdLight Academy
- EdLight News
- EdLight Initiative

Users should be able to ask Sandra questions about these initiatives and receive responses generated using indexed knowledge from EdLight repositories and documentation.

This PRD defines the **Version 1 implementation** of Sandra.

Version 1 focuses on:

- web chat interface
- knowledge retrieval from EdLight repositories
- structured agent runtime
- tool-based architecture
- secure system foundation

This version **does not yet include messaging platforms or private user data access**.

---

# 2. Implementation Goal

The goal of Version 1 is to deliver a working Sandra assistant that can:

- run as a web-based chat interface
- answer questions about EdLight initiatives
- retrieve answers using indexed repository documentation
- maintain conversation sessions
- expose a structured runtime and tool architecture
- provide administrative indexing controls

Sandra Version 1 should be deployable and usable by end users through the web interface.

---

# 3. In Scope

The following features **must be implemented in Version 1**.

## Web Chat Interface
Users can open Sandra in a browser and interact through a chat UI.

## Public Knowledge Question Answering
Sandra can answer questions about:

- EdLight Code
- EdLight News
- EdLight Academy
- EdLight Initiative

using indexed repository content.

## Repository Indexing
Sandra must index the following repositories:

- https://github.com/edlinitiative/code
- https://github.com/edlinitiative/EdLight-News
- https://github.com/edlinitiative/EdLight-Initiative
- https://github.com/edlinitiative/EdLight-Academy

README and documentation files must be processed into a retrieval system.

## Sandra Agent Runtime
Sandra must operate through a centralized runtime that:

- receives normalized user requests
- retrieves relevant knowledge
- executes tools when necessary
- generates responses
- logs actions

## Tool-Based Architecture
Sandra must access system functionality through a tool registry rather than direct database access.

## Admin Indexing Controls
Admins must be able to:

- list indexed repositories
- trigger indexing manually
- view indexing status

## Multilingual Foundation
Sandra must support:

- Haitian Creole
- French
- English

---

# 4. Out of Scope

The following features **must NOT be implemented in Version 1**.

- WhatsApp integration
- Instagram messaging
- email sending
- voice interaction
- private user data access
- course enrollment retrieval
- certificate retrieval
- autonomous multi-step agent workflows
- advanced analytics dashboards
- cross-platform workflow automation

These features are planned for later versions.

---

# 5. Required Technology Stack

The implementation must use the following technologies.

Frontend:

- Next.js (App Router)
- TypeScript
- Tailwind CSS

Backend:

- Next.js server routes
- Node.js runtime

Authentication:

- Firebase Auth

Realtime State:

- Firestore

Structured Database:

- Postgres
- Prisma ORM

Validation:

- Zod

AI Integration:

- LLM provider abstraction layer

Knowledge Retrieval:

- vector store abstraction

---

# 6. Required Repository Structure

The repository must follow this structure.

apps/
web/
admin/

services/
agent-runtime/
gateway-api/
indexer/
workers/

packages/
auth/
channels/
tools/
connectors/
memory/
knowledge/
config/
shared-types/

docs/

Explanation:

apps/web  
Web chat interface.

apps/admin  
Internal admin UI.

services/agent-runtime  
Sandra runtime orchestration.

services/gateway-api  
Internal API exposing tools.

services/indexer  
Repository indexing pipeline.

services/workers  
Background jobs.

packages/auth  
Authentication helpers.

packages/channels  
Channel adapters.

packages/tools  
Tool registry and execution.

packages/connectors  
System integrations.

packages/memory  
Conversation memory.

packages/knowledge  
Retrieval logic.

packages/config  
Environment configuration.

packages/shared-types  
Shared TypeScript types.

---

# 7. Core User Stories

## User Story 1

As a visitor,  
I want to ask Sandra questions about EdLight initiatives  
so that I can learn about available programs.

### Acceptance Criteria

- user opens Sandra web chat
- user submits question
- Sandra generates answer
- answer uses indexed knowledge when relevant
- conversation history is stored

---

## User Story 2

As a visitor,  
I want Sandra to remember the conversation during the session  
so that I can ask follow-up questions.

### Acceptance Criteria

- session ID created for each conversation
- message history stored
- follow-up questions reference prior context

---

## User Story 3

As an administrator,  
I want to trigger repository indexing  
so that Sandra can learn new documentation.

### Acceptance Criteria

- admin can list registered repositories
- admin can trigger indexing
- indexing status is visible

---

# 8. Data Model (MVP)

## Postgres Tables

Required tables:

users  
initiatives  
repo_registry  
indexed_sources  
indexed_documents  
index_jobs  
tool_executions  
audit_logs  

---

## Firestore Collections

chat_sessions  

Subcollection:

chat_sessions/{sessionId}/messages

---

# 9. Tool Contracts (MVP)

Sandra must implement the following tools.

## searchKnowledgeBase

Description  
Search indexed knowledge.

Required scope  
read:public_content

Input

{
“query”: “What is EdLight Code?”
}

Output

{
“results”: []
}

---

## getEdLightInitiatives

Description  
Return list of initiatives.

Required scope  
read:public_content

Input

{}

Output

{
“initiatives”: []
}

---

## lookupRepoInfo

Description  
Return repository metadata.

Required scope  
read:public_content

Input

{
“repoFullName”: “edlinitiative/code”
}

Output

{
“name”: “”,
“repoUrl”: “”
}

---

## triggerRepoIndexing

Description  
Trigger indexing job.

Required scope  
admin:indexing

Input

{
“repoId”: “”
}

Output

{
“jobId”: “”
}

---

## getIndexingStatus

Description  
Return indexing job status.

Required scope  
admin:indexing

Input

{
“jobId”: “”
}

Output

{
“status”: “”
}

---

# 10. API Contracts

## POST /api/sandra/chat

Main chat endpoint.

Input

{
“sessionId”: “”,
“message”: “”,
“language”: “en”
}

Output

{
“reply”: “”,
“sessionId”: “”,
“messageId”: “”
}

---

## GET /api/sandra/conversations/:sessionId

Returns session messages.

---

## GET /api/sandra/repos

Lists registered repositories.

---

## POST /api/sandra/repos/:repoId/index

Triggers indexing job.

---

## GET /api/sandra/index-jobs/:jobId

Returns job status.

---

## GET /api/sandra/health

Health check endpoint.

---

# 11. Repository Indexing Requirements

The indexing system must:

1. fetch repository README files
2. fetch documentation files
3. extract text
4. chunk documents
5. generate embeddings
6. store vector references
7. track metadata in Postgres

Indexed content must be retrievable by the Sandra runtime.

---

# 12. Security Constraints

The implementation must enforce the following rules.

- Sandra runtime must not query raw databases directly
- all database access must go through services
- tool execution must enforce permission scopes
- tool calls must be logged
- admin actions must be logged
- environment secrets must never be exposed to client code

---

# 13. Implementation Milestones

The system must be implemented in this order.

1. repository structure and config
2. shared types and environment validation
3. Firebase Auth integration
4. Prisma/Postgres schema
5. Firestore chat persistence
6. Sandra runtime
7. tool registry
8. GitHub indexing pipeline
9. retrieval system
10. API routes
11. web chat UI
12. admin indexing interface
13. documentation

---

# 14. Deliverables

The following components must exist after implementation.

- working web chat assistant
- repository indexing pipeline
- retrieval-based responses
- tool registry system
- admin indexing controls
- Firebase authentication
- Firestore session storage
- Postgres database schema
- documented architecture

---

# 15. Future Versions

Future versions will include:

- authenticated user data tools
- WhatsApp integration
- Instagram integration
- email assistant
- voice interaction
- personalized learning guidance
- agent workflows

These features are intentionally excluded from Version 1.

---

# 16. Success Criteria

Sandra Version 1 will be considered successful when:

- users can chat with Sandra through the web interface
- Sandra answers questions about EdLight initiatives
- responses use indexed repository knowledge
- conversations persist during sessions
- admins can manage repository indexing
- the architecture supports future expansion

1→# Sandra — Architecture
     2→
     3→## System Overview
     4→
     5→Sandra is built as a Next.js application with a modular library layer that contains all business logic. The architecture follows clean separation of concerns: UI components never contain business logic, and the library layer is framework-agnostic where possible.
     6→
     7→```
     8→┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
     9→│              Next.js App Layer                │
    10→│  ┌────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐ │
    11→│  │ Pages  │  │ API      │  │ Server       │ │
    12→│  │ (React)│  │ Routes   │  │ Actions      │ │
    13→│  └───┬────┘  └────┬─────┘  └──────┬───────┘ │
    14→│      │            │               │          │
    15→│      └────────────┼───────────────┘          │
    16→│                   │                          │
    17→│   ┌───────────────▼──────────────────────┐   │
    18→│   │         Library Layer (src/lib/)     │   │
    19→│   │                                      │   │
    20→│   │  ┌─────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐   │   │
    21→│   │  │ Agents  │  │  Knowledge/RAG   │   │   │
    22→│   │  │         │  │  - Chunker       │   │   │
    23→│   │  │ - Sandra│  │  - Embeddings    │   │   │
    24→│   │  │ - Prompt│  │  - Vector Store  │   │   │
    25→│   │  │ - Tools │  │  - Retrieval     │   │   │
    26→│   │  └─────────┘  └──────────────────┘   │   │
    27→│   │                                      │   │
    28→│   │  ┌─────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐   │   │
    29→│   │  │   AI    │  │    Channels      │   │   │
    30→│   │  │Provider │  │  - Web           │   │   │
    31→│   │  │- OpenAI │  │  - WhatsApp      │   │   │
    32→│   │  │- (...)  │  │  - Instagram     │   │   │
    33→│   │  └─────────┘  │  - Email/Voice   │   │   │
    34→│   │               └──────────────────┘   │   │
    35→│   │                                      │   │
    36→│   │  ┌─────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐   │   │
    37→│   │  │ Memory  │  │    GitHub        │   │   │
    38→│   │  │- Session│  │  - Client        │   │   │
    39→│   │  │- User   │  │  - Fetcher       │   │   │
    40→│   │  └─────────┘  │  - Indexer       │   │   │
    41→│   │               └──────────────────┘   │   │
    42→│   │                                      │   │
    43→│   │  ┌─────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐   │   │
    44→│   │  │ Config  │  │    Utils         │   │   │
    45→│   │  │ - Env   │  │  - Errors        │   │   │
    46→│   │  │ - Const │  │  - Logger        │   │   │
    47→│   │  └─────────┘  │  - Validation    │   │   │
    48→│   │               └──────────────────┘   │   │
    49→│   └──────────────────────────────────────┘   │
    50→│                                              │
    51→│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐   │
    52→│   │            Data Layer                │   │
    53→│   │  Prisma + PostgreSQL  │  Vector Store│   │
    54→│   └──────────────────────────────────────┘   │
    55→└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    56→```
    57→
    58→## Key Design Decisions
    59→
    60→### 1. Provider Abstraction
    61→All AI functionality goes through the `AIProvider` interface. The OpenAI implementation is the first, but Anthropic and Google can be added by implementing the same interface. No code outside `src/lib/ai/` knows which provider is being used.
    62→
    63→### 2. Agent Loop Pattern
    64→Sandra uses a **ReAct-style agent loop**:
    65→1. Receive input
    66→2. Build context (memory, retrieval, tools)
    67→3. Call LLM
    68→4. If tool calls → execute → loop back to step 3
    69→5. Return final response
    70→
    71→This pattern is proven at scale and allows Sandra to evolve from a simple Q&A bot to a full agent that takes actions.
    72→
    73→### 3. Memory Architecture
    74→Two tiers:
    75→- **Session memory** — Short-term conversation context. Lives in memory or Redis.
    76→- **User memory** — Long-term facts about users. Persisted in PostgreSQL.
    77→
    78→Both are behind interfaces so the storage backend can be swapped without changing the agent.
    79→
    80→### 4. Knowledge/RAG Pipeline
    81→Documents flow through: **Fetch → Chunk → Embed → Store → Retrieve**
    82→
    83→Each step is a separate function/class, making it easy to:
    84→- Change chunking strategies
    85→- Swap embedding models
    86→- Replace the vector store
    87→- Add new document sources
    88→
    89→### 5. Channel Normalization
    90→Every channel adapter converts platform-specific payloads into a standard `InboundMessage` format. This means the agent doesn't need to know whether a message came from WhatsApp or the web.
    91→
    92→### 6. Tool Registry
    93→Tools are self-registering: they import the registry and add themselves. The agent reads from the registry at runtime. Adding a new tool is: create file → implement interface → import in index.ts.
    94→
    95→## Data Flow: Chat Message
    96→
    97→```
    98→User types message in web UI
    99→        │
   100→        ▼
   101→POST /api/chat { message, sessionId, language }
   102→        │
   103→        ▼
   104→Zod validation
   105→        │
   106→        ▼
   107→runSandraAgent(input)
   108→        │
   109→        ├── Load session memory (conversation history)
   110→        ├── Load user memory (long-term facts)
   111→        ├── Retrieve relevant knowledge (vector search)
   112→        ├── Build system prompt
   113→        │
   114→        ▼
   115→AI Provider: chatCompletion(messages + tools)
   116→        │
   117→        ├── If tool_calls: execute tools → loop
   118→        │
   119→        ▼
   120→Return response
   121→        │
   122→        ├── Save to session memory
   123→        │
   124→        ▼
   125→JSON response to client
   126→```
   127→
   128→## Configuration
   129→
   130→All configuration is environment-variable driven with Zod validation at startup. The `env.ts` module fails fast in production if required variables are missing, and uses safe defaults in development.
   131→
   132→## Error Handling
   133→
   134→All errors flow through `SandraError` subclasses. API routes catch errors and return structured JSON responses with error codes. The logger provides structured output for debugging.
   135→
   136→## Scalability Path
   137→
   138→1. **Vector Store**: Replace `InMemoryVectorStore` with Pinecone/Qdrant/pgvector
   139→2. **Memory**: Replace in-memory stores with Redis (session) and PostgreSQL (user)
   140→3. **Indexing**: Add a job queue (BullMQ) for background indexing
   141→4. **Channels**: Add webhook endpoints for WhatsApp/Instagram
   142→5. **Auth**: Add NextAuth.js for user authentication
   143→6. **Monitoring**: Add OpenTelemetry for observability
   144→
   145→
   146→# Sandra — Full System Architecture
   147→
   148→Organization: EdLight Initiative  
   149→Project: Sandra  
   150→Document Type: System Architecture  
   151→Status: Draft  
   152→Version: 1.0  
   153→
   154→---
   155→
   156→# 1. Purpose
   157→
   158→Sandra is the central AI assistant and future agent layer for the EdLight ecosystem.
   159→
   160→She is designed to become the unified conversational interface across EdLight platforms, allowing users to interact with EdLight services through natural language instead of navigating multiple websites or systems manually.
   161→
   162→Sandra should support:
   163→
   164→- Web chat
   165→- WhatsApp
   166→- Instagram
   167→- Email
   168→- Live voice interactions
   169→- Haitian Creole, French, and English
   170→- Knowledge retrieval across EdLight repositories and documentation
   171→- Secure access to user-specific data when authorized
   172→- Tool-based actions across EdLight systems
   173→
   174→This architecture is designed to support both:
   175→
   176→- a fast MVP
   177→- a scalable long-term platform
   178→
   179→---
   180→
   181→# 2. High-Level Architecture
   182→
   183→Sandra should be built as a layered system.
   184→
   185→## Core Layers
   186→
   187→1. Channel Layer  
   188→2. Identity and Access Layer  
   189→3. Agent Runtime Layer  
   190→4. Tool and Gateway Layer  
   191→5. Knowledge and Retrieval Layer  
   192→6. Data Layer  
   193→7. Integration Layer  
   194→8. Worker and Automation Layer  
   195→9. Admin and Observability Layer  
   196→
   197→The central architectural principle is:
   198→
   199→**All channels feed into one Sandra runtime, and Sandra interacts with systems only through controlled tools and internal services.**
   200→
   201→Sandra must not be designed as a collection of disconnected bots.  
   202→She should be one core system with multiple interfaces.
   203→
   204→---
   205→
   206→# 3. Architectural Principles
   207→
   208→## 3.1 One Brain, Many Channels
   209→
   210→Sandra should have one shared agent runtime and one shared tool layer.
   211→
   212→Web chat, WhatsApp, Instagram, email, and voice should all send normalized requests into the same core system.
   213→
   214→This ensures:
   215→
   216→- consistent behavior
   217→- shared memory
   218→- easier maintenance
   219→- easier rollout of new capabilities
   220→
   221→---
   222→
   223→## 3.2 Tool-Based System Access
   224→
   225→Sandra should never be given unrestricted access to raw databases or random services.
   226→
   227→Sandra should interact with systems through structured tools such as:
   228→
   229→- `getUserCertificates`
   230→- `getEnrollmentStatus`
   231→- `getLatestNews`
   232→- `searchKnowledgeBase`
   233→- `getProgramDetails`
   234→- `sendWhatsappReply`
   235→- `draftEmail`
   236→
   237→This keeps the system secure, auditable, and easier to evolve.
   238→
   239→---
   240→
   241→## 3.3 Public Knowledge and Private Data Must Be Separate
   242→
   243→Sandra should support two different access modes:
   244→
   245→### Public or system-level mode
   246→Used for:
   247→
   248→- FAQs
   249→- public course information
   250→- public program details
   251→- public news summaries
   252→- public EdLight documentation
   253→
   254→### User-authorized mode
   255→Used for:
   256→
   257→- user certificates
   258→- user enrollments
   259→- application status
   260→- user preferences
   261→- private messages
   262→- personalized guidance
   263→
   264→Private data should only be accessed when the user is authenticated and authorized.
   265→
   266→---
   267→
   268→## 3.4 Fast MVP, Strong Long-Term Foundation
   269→
   270→The MVP should be easy to ship.  
   271→The long-term design should not force a rewrite.
   272→
   273→That means:
   274→
   275→- modular connectors
   276→- modular tools
   277→- a clean gateway layer
   278→- a clear split between realtime app data and structured business data
   279→
   280→---

   341→  # Sandra — Data Model and API/Tool Contract Specification
   342→
   343→Organization: EdLight Initiative  
   344→Project: Sandra  
   345→Document Type: Data Model and Interface Specification  
   346→Status: Draft  
   347→Version: 1.0  
   348→
   349→---
   350→
   351→# 1. Purpose
   352→
   353→This document defines the core data model for Sandra and the contracts between:
   354→
   355→- Sandra Runtime
   356→- Sandra Gateway API
   357→- internal tools
   358→- connectors
   359→- EdLight systems
   360→- channel adapters
   361→
   362→It is designed to complement the main architecture document.
   363→
   364→This specification covers:
   365→
   366→- Postgres data model
   367→- Firestore data model
   368→- vector knowledge model
   369→- tool contract design
   370→- API contract design
   371→- permission model
   372→- audit model
   373→
   374→The goal is to ensure Sandra can scale from a simple assistant into a secure multi-system AI platform for the EdLight ecosystem.
   375→
   376→---
   377→
   378→# 2. Core Design Principles
   379→
   380→## 2.1 Source-of-Truth Separation
   381→
   382→Sandra should not store all data in one place.
   383→
   384→Different categories of data should live in the storage layer best suited for them:
   385→
   386→- Firestore for realtime conversation and client-facing interaction state
   387→- Postgres for structured relational business and operational data
   388→- Vector store for unstructured semantic knowledge retrieval
   389→
   390→---
   391→
   392→## 2.2 Tool-Mediated Data Access
   393→
   394→Sandra Runtime must not directly access raw tables or collections.
   395→
   396→All sensitive and structured data access should happen through:
   397→
   398→- internal APIs
   399→- tool contracts
   400→- connectors
   401→
   402→This ensures:
   403→
   404→- cleaner architecture
   405→- strict permission enforcement
   406→- observability
   407→- easier future refactoring
   408→
   409→---
   410→
   411→## 2.3 Public and Private Data Separation
   412→
   413→Sandra must clearly separate:
   414→
   415→### Public data
   416→Examples:
   417→
   418→- public initiative descriptions
   419→- public course catalog
   420→- public FAQs
   421→- repo documentation
   422→- public news content
   423→
   424→### Private data
   425→Examples:
   426→
   427→- user enrollments
   428→- certificates
   429→- application status
   430→- contact details
   431→- consent records
   432→- channel connection metadata
   433→
   434→Private data should only be accessible with valid identity and permission checks.
   435→
   436→---
   437→
   438→# 3. Canonical Identity Model
   439→
   440→Sandra should maintain a canonical user model in Postgres, while Firebase Auth acts as the main authentication provider.
   441→
   442→## Identity Components
   443→
   444→### Firebase Auth
   445→Used for:
   446→
   447→- sign-in
   448→- token issuance
   449→- session authentication
   450→- role claims for app clients
   451→
   452→### Postgres Canonical User
   453→Used for:
   454→
   455→- persistent user record
   456→- cross-system identity mapping
   457→- platform relationships
   458→- internal permissions
   459→- operational logic
   460→
   461→## Identity Flow
   462→
   463→1. user authenticates through Firebase Auth  
   464→2. backend validates Firebase token  
   465→3. backend maps Firebase UID to canonical user in Postgres  
   466→4. Sandra Runtime uses canonical user ID internally  
   467→
   468→This allows Sandra to keep one internal identity even if users interact across multiple EdLight systems.
   469→
   470→---
   471→
   472→# 4. Postgres Data Model
   473→
   474→Postgres is the canonical structured data layer.
   475→
   476→## 4.1 `users`
   477→

   479→

   482→- `id` UUID primary key
   483→- `firebase_uid` text unique nullable
   484→- `email` text unique nullable
   485→- `phone_number` text nullable
   486→- `full_name` text nullable
   487→- `preferred_language` text nullable
   488→- `country` text nullable
   489→- `timezone` text nullable
   490→- `status` text not null default `active`
   491→- `created_at` timestamp not null
   492→- `updated_at` timestamp not null
   493→
   494→### Notes
   495→
   496→This is the main record used across Sandra tools and connectors.

   745→## 4.13 `consents`

   747→Stores user consent records.
   748→
   749→### Fields
   750→
   751→- `id` UUID primary key
   752→- `user_id` UUID foreign key
   753→- `consent_type` text not null
   754→- `granted` boolean not null
   755→- `granted_at` timestamp nullable
   756→- `revoked_at` timestamp nullable
   757→- `metadata` jsonb nullable
   758→- `created_at` timestamp not null
   759→- `updated_at` timestamp not null
   760→
   761→### Example consent types
   762→
   763→- `email_access`
   764→- `whatsapp_messaging`
   765→- `personalized_recommendations`
   766→- `data_processing`
   767→- `channel_linking`
   768→

   771→## 4.14 `connector_registrations`

   807→- `expires_at` timestamp nullable

   818→
   819→## 4.16 `repo_registry`

   987→# 5. Firestore Data Model
   988→
   989→Firestore is for realtime and client-facing conversational state.
   990→
   991→## 5.1 `chat_sessions`
   992→
   993→Represents active or archived chat sessions.
   994→
   995→### Fields
   996→
   997→- `sessionId`
   998→- `userId` nullable
   999→- `channel`
  1000→- `status`
  1001→- `language`
  1002→- `title` nullable
  1003→- `lastMessageAt`
  1004→- `createdAt`
  1005→- `updatedAt`
  1006→- `metadata`
  1007→
  1008→### Example channel values
  1009→
  1010→- `web`
  1011→- `whatsapp`
  1012→- `instagram`
  1013→- `email`
  1014→- `voice`
  1015→
  1016→### Example status values
  1017→
  1018→- `active`
  1019→- `closed`
  1020→- `archived`
  1021→
  1022→---
  1023→
  1024→## 5.2 `chat_sessions/{sessionId}/messages`
  1025→

  1034→- `senderType`
  1035→- `language`
  1036→- `timestamp`
  1037→- `status`
  1038→- `toolCalls` optional
  1039→- `metadata`
  1040→
  1041→### Example roles

  1045→- `system`
  1046→- `tool`

  1120→
  1121→# 6. Vector Knowledge Model
  1122→
  1123→The vector layer stores semantic representations of content Sandra can retrieve.
  1124→
  1125→## Knowledge Objects
  1126→
  1127→Every indexed chunk should be associated with:
  1128→
  1129→- `vector_id`
  1130→- `source_id`
  1131→- `document_key`
  1132→- `chunk_index`
  1133→- `content`
  1134→- `source_type`
  1135→- `initiative`
  1136→- `language`
  1137→- `tags`
  1138→- `last_indexed_at`
  1139→
  1140→## Recommended Metadata Dimensions
  1141→
  1142→- initiative name
  1143→- repo full name
  1144→- source path
  1145→- content type
  1146→- public or private visibility
  1147→- language
  1148→- tags
  1149→- version or checksum
  1150→
  1151→## Initial Sources to Index
  1152→
  1153→- repo READMEs
  1154→- docs folders
  1155→- public initiative pages
  1156→- FAQs
  1157→- course descriptions
  1158→- program descriptions
  1159→- policies
  1160→
  1161→---
  1162→
  1163→# 7. API Design Principles
  1164→
  1165→Sandra should expose internal APIs through the Sandra Gateway.
  1166→
  1167→## API Characteristics
  1168→
  1169→- server-side only for sensitive operations
  1170→- input validation required
  1171→- output should be structured and typed
  1172→- permission checks required
  1173→- auditable for sensitive actions
  1174→
  1175→## API Categories
  1176→
  1177→### Public Data APIs
  1178→Examples:
  1179→
  1180→- list initiatives
  1181→- get public course catalog
  1182→- get program information
  1183→- search indexed public knowledge
  1184→
  1185→### User Data APIs
  1186→Examples:
  1187→
  1188→- get own certificates
  1189→- get own enrollments
  1190→- get own application status
  1191→- get preferences
  1192→
  1193→### Operational APIs
  1194→Examples:
  1195→
  1196→- list repos
  1197→- trigger indexing
  1198→- get indexing status
  1199→- view system health
  1200→
  1201→---
  1202→
  1203→# 8. Standard API Envelope
  1204→
  1205→All Sandra Gateway APIs should use a consistent response envelope.
  1206→
  1207→## Success Response
  1208→
  1209→```json
  1210→{
  1211→  "success": true,
  1212→  "data": {},
  1213→  "error": null,
  1214→  "meta": {
  1215→    "requestId": "req_123"
  1216→  }
  1217→}
  1218→
  1219→Error Response
  1220→
  1221→{
  1222→  "success": false,
  1223→  "data": null,
  1224→  "error": {
  1225→    "code": "FORBIDDEN",
  1226→    "message": "User is not authorized to access this resource."
  1227→  },
  1228→  "meta": {
  1229→    "requestId": "req_123"
  1230→  }
  1231→}
  1232→
  1233→
  1234→⸻
  1235→
  1236→9. Core Internal API Contracts
  1237→
  1238→9.1 POST /api/sandra/chat
  1239→
  1240→Main entry point for chat.
  1241→
  1242→Request

  1264→      }
  1265→    ]
  1266→  },
  1267→  "error": null,
  1268→  "meta": {

  1270→  }
  1271→}
