# Sandra V1 User Stories

## Summary

25 user stories across 6 categories.

## Traceability Matrix

| US ID | Title | Feature | Task(s) | Status |
|-------|-------|---------|---------|--------|
| US-01 | Database schema setup | F11 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-02 | Seed initial data | F11 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-03 | Receive responses in preferred language | F8 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-04 | Language types and validation | F8 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-05 | Structured error responses | F12 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-06 | Tool execution audit trail | F12 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-07 | Authenticate to access Sandra | F9 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-08 | Admin role verification | F9 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-09 | Conversation persists during session | F7 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-10 | Resume a conversation | F7 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-11 | Tool registry for agent functionality | F5 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-12 | Scoped tool execution | F5 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-13 | Agent processes user message | F4 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-14 | Agent uses tools when needed | F4 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-15 | Index repository documentation | F3 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-16 | Track indexing job progress | F3 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-17 | Ask about EdLight initiatives | F2 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-18 | Answer uses indexed knowledge | F2 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-19 | Chat API endpoint | F10 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-20 | Admin API endpoints | F10 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-21 | Chat with Sandra in browser | F1 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-22 | See streaming responses | F1 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-23 | Select language in chat UI | F1 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-24 | Trigger repository indexing | F6 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-25 | View indexing status | F6 | TBD | [ ] |

---

## Stories by Category

### Foundation & Infrastructure (US-01 through US-06)

#### US-01: Database Schema Setup

> As a developer, I want the Postgres database schema defined and migrated so that all services have reliable structured storage.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Prisma schema defines all MVP tables: users, initiatives, repo_registry, indexed_sources, indexed_documents, index_jobs, tool_executions, audit_logs
- [ ] Running `npx prisma migrate dev` succeeds and creates all tables
- [ ] Prisma client is generated with typed models for each table
- [ ] Environment config validates DATABASE_URL with Zod at startup
- [ ] Schema matches the data model in PRD section 8

**Feature:** F11 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-02: Seed Initial Data

> As a developer, I want the four EdLight repositories and initiative records pre-seeded so that the system has baseline data without manual entry.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Seed script creates repo_registry entries for edlinitiative/code, EdLight-News, EdLight-Initiative, EdLight-Academy
- [ ] Seed script creates initiative records for EdLight Code, EdLight News, EdLight Academy, EdLight Initiative
- [ ] Running the seed script is idempotent (re-running does not create duplicates)
- [ ] Seed script can be invoked via `npx prisma db seed`

**Feature:** F11 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-03: Receive Responses in Preferred Language

> As a visitor, I want to receive Sandra's responses in my preferred language (English, French, or Haitian Creole) so that I can understand the information.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Language parameter (en, fr, ht) is accepted on each chat request
- [ ] Sandra's system prompt instructs the LLM to respond in the requested language
- [ ] If no language is specified, Sandra defaults to English
- [ ] Language-specific prompt templates exist for all three supported languages

**Feature:** F8 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-04: Language Types and Validation

> As a developer, I want language enums, types, and Zod schemas defined so that language handling is consistent across the codebase.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] SupportedLanguage enum defines en, fr, ht values
- [ ] Zod schema validates language input and rejects unsupported values
- [ ] Language utility functions are exported from a single module (src/lib/config or src/lib/utils)
- [ ] Type definitions are reusable by API routes, agent runtime, and UI components

**Feature:** F8 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-05: Structured Error Responses

> As a developer, I want all errors to flow through SandraError subclasses so that API consumers receive consistent, actionable error messages.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] SandraError base class exists with code, message, and statusCode properties
- [ ] Subclasses exist: ValidationError (400), AuthError (401), ForbiddenError (403), NotFoundError (404), ToolError (500)
- [ ] API routes catch SandraError instances and return structured JSON with error code and message
- [ ] Unhandled errors are caught and returned as generic 500 responses without leaking internals

**Feature:** F12 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-06: Tool Execution Audit Trail

> As an administrator, I want all tool executions and admin actions logged so that I can audit system behavior.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Every tool execution writes a record to the tool_executions table with tool name, input, output, user context, and timestamp
- [ ] Admin actions (trigger indexing, etc.) write records to the audit_logs table
- [ ] Audit records include the request ID for traceability
- [ ] Logging does not block the main request flow (failures are caught and logged, not re-thrown)

**Feature:** F12 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

### Authentication & Sessions (US-07 through US-10)

#### US-07: Authenticate to Access Sandra

> As a visitor, I want to optionally sign in with Firebase Auth so that my identity is recognized for session continuity.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Firebase Admin SDK is initialized on the server with validated environment config
- [ ] API routes accept a Bearer token in the Authorization header
- [ ] Valid Firebase tokens are decoded and the user's Firebase UID is extracted
- [ ] If a Postgres user record does not exist for the Firebase UID, one is created (user resolution flow)
- [ ] Unauthenticated requests to public endpoints (chat, health) are allowed without a token

**Feature:** F9 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-08: Admin Role Verification

> As an administrator, I want admin-only endpoints to verify my admin role so that unauthorized users cannot trigger indexing or access admin features.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Admin role is stored on the Postgres user record
- [ ] Auth middleware checks the admin role before allowing access to admin endpoints
- [ ] Non-admin authenticated users receive a 403 Forbidden response on admin endpoints
- [ ] Unauthenticated users receive a 401 Unauthorized response on admin endpoints

**Feature:** F9 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-09: Conversation Persists During Session

> As a visitor, I want my conversation with Sandra to persist during the session so that I can scroll back and see previous messages.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] A new session ID is generated when a conversation starts
- [ ] Each message (user and assistant) is stored in Firestore at chat_sessions/{sessionId}/messages
- [ ] Messages are stored with role, content, timestamp, and messageId
- [ ] Retrieving a session returns messages in chronological order
- [ ] Session data is accessible for the duration of the conversation

**Feature:** F7 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-10: Resume a Conversation

> As a visitor, I want to resume a previous conversation by providing a session ID so that I can continue asking follow-up questions.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Providing an existing session ID in a chat request loads prior message history
- [ ] The agent receives recent message history as context for generating responses
- [ ] Follow-up questions correctly reference information from earlier in the conversation
- [ ] Invalid or non-existent session IDs result in a new session being created

**Feature:** F7 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

### Agent Core (US-11 through US-14)

#### US-11: Tool Registry for Agent Functionality

> As a developer, I want a self-registering tool registry so that the agent can discover and invoke tools at runtime without hardcoded references.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Tool interface defines name, description, inputSchema (Zod), requiredScopes, and handler
- [ ] Tool registry provides register() and getAll() methods
- [ ] Each MVP tool (searchKnowledgeBase, getEdLightInitiatives, lookupRepoInfo, triggerRepoIndexing, getIndexingStatus) self-registers on import
- [ ] Registry exports tool definitions in a format consumable by the LLM (name, description, parameters)
- [ ] Adding a new tool requires only: create file, implement interface, import in index

**Feature:** F5 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-12: Scoped Tool Execution

> As a developer, I want tool execution to enforce permission scopes so that public tools and admin tools have different access levels.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Permission scopes are defined: read:public_content, admin:indexing
- [ ] Tool executor checks the caller's scopes against the tool's requiredScopes before execution
- [ ] Public tools (searchKnowledgeBase, getEdLightInitiatives, lookupRepoInfo) require read:public_content
- [ ] Admin tools (triggerRepoIndexing, getIndexingStatus) require admin:indexing
- [ ] Scope violations return a ForbiddenError with a descriptive message

**Feature:** F5 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-13: Agent Processes User Message

> As a developer, I want the Sandra agent runtime to receive a user message, build context, call the LLM, and return a response so that all channels use a single processing path.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Agent runtime accepts an InboundMessage (content, sessionId, language, user context)
- [ ] Runtime loads session history from Firestore and builds a context window
- [ ] System prompt includes language instruction, available tools, and session context
- [ ] LLM is called via the AIProvider abstraction (OpenAI implementation)
- [ ] Final response is returned as an OutboundMessage with content and metadata
- [ ] Agent actions (LLM calls, tool executions) are logged

**Feature:** F4 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-14: Agent Uses Tools When Needed

> As a developer, I want the agent to detect when a tool call is needed, execute it, and incorporate the result so that Sandra can answer questions requiring data retrieval.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] When the LLM response includes a tool call, the runtime executes the tool via the registry
- [ ] Tool results are fed back into the LLM for the next iteration of the agent loop
- [ ] The agent loop continues until the LLM produces a final text response (no more tool calls)
- [ ] A maximum iteration limit prevents infinite loops (e.g., 5 iterations)
- [ ] Tool call results are included in the session message history

**Feature:** F4 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

### Knowledge Pipeline (US-15 through US-18)

#### US-15: Index Repository Documentation

> As an administrator, I want to index README and documentation files from EdLight repositories so that Sandra can answer questions using up-to-date content.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] GitHub API client fetches README.md and files in docs/ directories for a given repository
- [ ] Fetched markdown content is extracted as plain text
- [ ] Documents are split into chunks using recursive character splitting with markdown-aware boundaries
- [ ] Each chunk is embedded using the embedding service (OpenAI text-embedding-3-small)
- [ ] Embeddings are stored in the vector store with source metadata (repo, file path, chunk index)
- [ ] Metadata is recorded in indexed_sources and indexed_documents Postgres tables

**Feature:** F3 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-16: Track Indexing Job Progress

> As an administrator, I want indexing jobs tracked with status updates so that I know when indexing completes or fails.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Triggering an index creates an index_jobs record with status "pending"
- [ ] Status transitions through pending → running → completed or failed
- [ ] Completed jobs record the number of documents and chunks indexed
- [ ] Failed jobs record the error message
- [ ] Job status is queryable by job ID

**Feature:** F3 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-17: Ask About EdLight Initiatives

> As a visitor, I want to ask Sandra questions about EdLight Code, EdLight News, EdLight Academy, and EdLight Initiative so that I can learn about available programs.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Asking "What is EdLight Code?" returns a response that references indexed EdLight Code documentation
- [ ] Asking "What programs does EdLight offer?" returns information about multiple initiatives
- [ ] Responses are conversational and accurate based on indexed content
- [ ] If no relevant knowledge is found, Sandra indicates it does not have that information rather than hallucinating

**Feature:** F2 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-18: Answer Uses Indexed Knowledge

> As a visitor, I want Sandra's answers to be grounded in indexed repository content so that the information is accurate and verifiable.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] The retrieval service converts the user query into an embedding and performs similarity search
- [ ] Top-k relevant chunks are injected into the agent's context window
- [ ] The LLM prompt instructs the model to answer based on provided context
- [ ] Retrieved chunks include source metadata (repository name, file path)
- [ ] The searchKnowledgeBase tool is wired to the retrieval service and invoked by the agent

**Feature:** F2 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

### API Layer (US-19 through US-20)

#### US-19: Chat API Endpoint

> As a developer, I want a POST /api/sandra/chat endpoint so that the web UI and future channels can send messages to Sandra through a standard API.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] POST /api/sandra/chat accepts { sessionId, message, language } with Zod validation
- [ ] Endpoint returns { reply, sessionId, messageId } in the standard JSON envelope
- [ ] Streaming responses are supported via SSE (Server-Sent Events) when Accept header requests it
- [ ] GET /api/sandra/conversations/:sessionId returns the message history for a session
- [ ] GET /api/sandra/health returns { status: "ok" } with service version
- [ ] All responses include a request ID for tracing

**Feature:** F10 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-20: Admin API Endpoints

> As an administrator, I want API endpoints for repository management so that admin UI and scripts can manage indexing.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] GET /api/sandra/repos returns the list of registered repositories with their indexing status
- [ ] POST /api/sandra/repos/:repoId/index triggers an indexing job and returns the job ID
- [ ] GET /api/sandra/index-jobs/:jobId returns the current status of an indexing job
- [ ] All admin endpoints require a valid Firebase Auth token with admin role
- [ ] Unauthorized requests receive a 401 or 403 response

**Feature:** F10 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

### User Interface (US-21 through US-25)

#### US-21: Chat With Sandra in Browser

> As a visitor, I want to open Sandra in my browser and type messages so that I can ask questions about EdLight.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] A chat page is accessible at the Sandra web URL
- [ ] The page displays a message input field and a send button
- [ ] Submitted messages appear in the chat history immediately
- [ ] Sandra's responses appear in the chat history after processing
- [ ] An empty/welcome state displays branding and suggested starter questions
- [ ] The layout is responsive and works on mobile and desktop browsers

**Feature:** F1 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-22: See Streaming Responses

> As a visitor, I want to see Sandra's response appear word-by-word as it generates so that I know the system is working and I can start reading immediately.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Responses stream to the UI token-by-token as they are generated by the LLM
- [ ] A typing/loading indicator is shown while waiting for the first token
- [ ] If streaming fails mid-response, an error message is displayed
- [ ] The chat input is disabled while a response is being generated
- [ ] Completed responses are stored in message history and render identically to non-streamed messages

**Feature:** F1 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-23: Select Language in Chat UI

> As a visitor, I want to select my preferred language (English, French, or Haitian Creole) in the chat interface so that Sandra responds in my language.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] A language selector is visible in the chat UI (dropdown or toggle)
- [ ] Selecting a language sends it as the language parameter on subsequent chat requests
- [ ] The selected language persists for the duration of the session
- [ ] The default language is English

**Feature:** F1 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-24: Trigger Repository Indexing

> As an administrator, I want to click a button to trigger indexing for a repository so that Sandra's knowledge base stays current.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Admin page displays a list of registered repositories
- [ ] Each repository row has an "Index Now" button
- [ ] Clicking the button calls POST /api/sandra/repos/:repoId/index
- [ ] A success message confirms the indexing job was started
- [ ] The button is disabled while an indexing job is already running for that repository
- [ ] Only authenticated admin users can access the admin page

**Feature:** F6 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

#### US-25: View Indexing Status

> As an administrator, I want to see the current indexing status for each repository so that I know which repositories are up to date.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Each repository in the admin list shows its last indexed timestamp
- [ ] Active indexing jobs show a progress indicator (pending, running, completed, failed)
- [ ] Failed jobs display the error reason
- [ ] The status updates without requiring a full page refresh (polling or realtime)

**Feature:** F6 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

    24→    {
    25→      "id": "F5",
    26→      "name": "Tool-Based Architecture",
    27→      "description": "Tool registry system where Sandra accesses functionality through self-registering tools rather than direct database access. Each tool defines name, description, inputSchema, requiredScopes, and handler. MVP tools: searchKnowledgeBase, getEdLightInitiatives, lookupRepoInfo, triggerRepoIndexing, getIndexingStatus."
    28→    },
    29→    {
    30→      "id": "F6",
    31→      "name": "Admin Indexing Controls",
    32→      "description": "Admin interface allowing administrators to list indexed repositories, trigger indexing manually, and view indexing status. Exposed through admin UI and API endpoints (GET /api/sandra/repos, POST /api/sandra/repos/:repoId/index, GET /api/sandra/index-jobs/:jobId)."
    33→    },
    34→    {
    35→      "id": "F7",
    36→      "name": "Conversation Session Management",
    37→      "description": "Session-based conversation persistence using Firestore. Each conversation gets a session ID, message history is stored in chat_sessions/{sessionId}/messages subcollection, and follow-up questions reference prior context."
    38→    },
    39→    {
    40→      "id": "F8",
    41→      "name": "Multilingual Foundation",
    42→      "description": "Language support for Haitian Creole, French, and English. Includes language enums, response language selection, user language preference handling, and system prompt helpers for multilingual behavior. Language is passed per request."
    43→    },
    44→    {
    45→      "id": "F9",
    46→      "name": "Authentication Integration",
    47→      "description": "Firebase Auth integration for user identity. Includes token verification on backend, canonical user resolution flow (Firebase UID to Postgres user), and user session helpers. Admin actions require authenticated admin role."
    48→    },
    49→    {
    50→      "id": "F10",
    51→      "name": "API Gateway",
    52→      "description": "REST API layer with endpoints: POST /api/sandra/chat, GET /api/sandra/conversations/:sessionId, GET /api/sandra/repos, POST /api/sandra/repos/:repoId/index, GET /api/sandra/index-jobs/:jobId, GET /api/sandra/health. All use standard success/error JSON envelope with request IDs."
    53→    },
    54→    {
    55→      "id": "F11",
    56→      "name": "Database Schema",
    57→      "description": "Postgres schema via Prisma ORM with MVP tables: users, initiatives, repo_registry, indexed_sources, indexed_documents, index_jobs, tool_executions, audit_logs. Firestore for chat_sessions and messages subcollection."
    58→    },
    59→    {
    60→      "id": "F12",
    61→      "name": "Security and Audit Layer",
    62→      "description": "Permission scope enforcement on tool execution (read:public_content, admin:indexing), tool call logging, admin action logging, environment secrets protection from client code, and structured error handling via SandraError subclasses."
    63→    }
    64→  ],
    65→  "personas": [
    66→    {
    67→      "name": "Visitor",
    68→      "description": "General public user who opens Sandra in a browser to ask questions about EdLight initiatives, programs, and services. May speak Haitian Creole, French, or English. Expects conversational Q&A with session continuity."
    69→    },
    70→    {
    71→      "name": "Administrator",
    72→      "description": "EdLight team member who manages the knowledge base. Needs to register repositories, trigger indexing, monitor indexing status, and ensure Sandra has up-to-date documentation. Accesses admin UI and admin API endpoints."
    73→    }
    74→  ],
    75→  "constraints": [
    76→    "Must use Next.js App Router with TypeScript and Tailwind CSS for frontend",
    77→    "Must use Next.js server routes and Node.js runtime for backend",
    78→    "Must use Firebase Auth for authentication",
    79→    "Must use Firestore for realtime chat session state",
    80→    "Must use Postgres with Prisma ORM for structured data",
    81→    "Must use Zod for input validation",
    82→    "Must use LLM provider abstraction layer (not tied to specific provider)",
    83→    "Must use vector store abstraction (not tied to specific vector DB)",
    84→    "Sandra runtime must not query raw databases directly — all access through services/tools",
    85→    "Tool execution must enforce permission scopes",
    86→    "All tool calls and admin actions must be logged",
    87→    "Environment secrets must never be exposed to client code",
    88→    "Must support Haitian Creole, French, and English",
    89→    "Must index four specific EdLight GitHub repositories",
    90→    "Implementation must follow the 13-milestone order defined in PRD section 13"
    91→  ],
    92→  "risks": [
    93→    {
    94→      "risk": "PRD specifies monorepo structure (apps/, services/, packages/) but existing codebase is a single Next.js app with src/lib/ — architectural mismatch",
    95→      "mitigation": "The architecture doc explicitly endorses the single Next.js app with library layer approach. Follow the existing src/lib/ structure which achieves the same separation. Document this decision clearly. The PRD structure can be seen as a logical grouping guide rather than a literal filesystem requirement."
    96→    },
    97→    {
    98→      "risk": "Vector store abstraction may limit retrieval quality without a production vector database",
    99→      "mitigation": "Start with in-memory vector store (already in codebase) for development. Design the abstraction so pgvector, Pinecone, or Qdrant can be swapped in without runtime changes. The architecture doc already identifies this as a scalability path."
   100→    },
   101→    {
   102→      "risk": "LLM provider abstraction adds complexity and may delay MVP",
   103→      "mitigation": "The existing codebase already has an AIProvider interface with OpenAI implementation. Use this pattern — implement OpenAI first, keep the abstraction thin. Only add providers when needed."
   104→    },
   105→    {
   106→      "risk": "Multilingual support (especially Haitian Creole) may have limited LLM quality",
   107→      "mitigation": "Rely on the LLM's built-in multilingual capabilities with clear system prompt instructions for language handling. Test Haitian Creole responses early. Consider language-specific prompt templates if quality is insufficient."
   108→    },
   109→    {
   110→      "risk": "GitHub rate limiting during repository indexing",
   111→      "mitigation": "Use authenticated GitHub API requests. Implement backoff and retry logic. Cache fetched content. Track checksums to avoid re-indexing unchanged content."
   112→    },
   113→    {
   114→      "risk": "Firestore and Postgres dual-database complexity increases operational burden",
   115→      "mitigation": "Clear separation of concerns: Firestore for realtime chat state only, Postgres for everything else. This is a deliberate architectural choice from the PRD. Keep the boundary strict to avoid confusion."
   116→    },
   117→    {
   118→      "risk": "Scope creep from the extensive architecture document which describes features beyond V1",
   119→      "mitigation": "Strictly scope to PRD V1 features only. The architecture and roadmap docs describe the full vision — use them for interface design decisions but do not implement post-V1 features (WhatsApp, Instagram, email, voice, private user data tools, autonomous workflows)."
   120→    }
   121→  ],
   122→  "non_goals": [
   123→    "WhatsApp integration",
   124→    "Instagram messaging",
   125→    "Email sending",
   126→    "Voice interaction",
   127→    "Private user data access (certificates, enrollments, application status)",
   128→    "Course enrollment retrieval",
   129→    "Certificate retrieval",
   130→    "Autonomous multi-step agent workflows",
   131→    "Advanced analytics dashboards",
   132→    "Cross-platform workflow automation",
   133→    "User memory / long-term personalization beyond session",
   134→    "Connector framework for external EdLight systems"
   135→  ],
   136→  "architecture_notes": [
   137→    "Single Next.js application with modular src/lib/ layer — NOT the monorepo structure in PRD section 6. The existing codebase and architecture doc confirm this approach.",
   138→    "ReAct-style agent loop: receive input → build context (memory, retrieval, tools) → call LLM → if tool calls, execute and loop → return final response",
   139→    "Two-tier data: Firestore for realtime chat sessions, Postgres for structured business data. Clear boundary — no mixing.",
   140→    "Tool registry pattern: tools self-register, agent reads registry at runtime. Adding a tool = create file + implement interface + import in index.ts.",
   141→    "Channel normalization: all adapters convert to standard InboundMessage format. V1 only implements web channel.",
   142→    "AIProvider interface abstracts LLM. OpenAI is first implementation. No code outside src/lib/ai/ knows which provider is used.",
   143→    "Environment config via Zod validation at startup — fail fast if required vars missing, safe defaults in dev.",
   144→    "All errors flow through SandraError subclasses. API routes return structured JSON with error codes.",
   145→    "Vector store abstraction: InMemoryVectorStore for MVP, designed for swap to pgvector/Pinecone/Qdrant.",
   146→    "Knowledge pipeline: Fetch → Chunk → Embed → Store → Retrieve — each step is a separate function/class."
   147→  ],
   148→  "adrs_created": [],
   149→  "adrs_reviewed": [],
   150→  "estimated_complexity": "large",
   151→  "technology_decisions": [
   152→    {
   153→      "area": "LLM Provider",
   154→      "chosen": "OpenAI (via provider abstraction)",
   155→      "alternatives_considered": ["Anthropic Claude", "Google Gemini"],
   156→      "rationale": "PRD requires an LLM provider abstraction layer. The existing codebase already implements an AIProvider interface with OpenAI as first provider. OpenAI has the broadest tool-calling support and multilingual capability. The abstraction allows swapping providers later without code changes outside src/lib/ai/."
   157→    },
   158→    {
   159→      "area": "Vector Store",
   160→      "chosen": "In-memory vector store (MVP) with abstraction for production swap",
   161→      "alternatives_considered": ["pgvector (Postgres extension)", "Pinecone", "Qdrant"],
   162→      "rationale": "PRD requires vector store abstraction. For MVP, in-memory store minimizes infrastructure. The architecture doc explicitly lists pgvector/Pinecone/Qdrant as scalability paths. The abstraction interface is already in the codebase. pgvector is the most natural upgrade since Postgres is already required."
   163→    },
   164→    {
   165→      "area": "Embedding Model",
   166→      "chosen": "OpenAI text-embedding-3-small",
   167→      "alternatives_considered": ["OpenAI text-embedding-3-large", "Cohere embed-v3"],
   168→      "rationale": "Using the same provider for LLM and embeddings simplifies API key management and billing. text-embedding-3-small offers good quality at lower cost for an MVP with four repositories. Can upgrade to large model if retrieval quality needs improvement."
   169→    },
   170→    {
   171→      "area": "Session Memory Storage",
   172→      "chosen": "Firestore (as specified by PRD)",
   173→      "alternatives_considered": ["Redis", "Postgres"],
   174→      "rationale": "PRD explicitly requires Firestore for chat sessions. Firestore provides realtime updates for the web chat UI and natural subcollection structure for messages. The architecture doc confirms this as the right layer for conversational state."
   175→    },
   176→    {
   177→      "area": "Background Job Processing",
   178→      "chosen": "Synchronous execution in API routes (MVP)",
   179→      "alternatives_considered": ["BullMQ with Redis", "Inngest", "Trigger.dev"],
   180→      "rationale": "V1 indexes only four repositories on manual admin trigger. Synchronous processing is sufficient. The architecture doc suggests BullMQ for future scaling. Adding a job queue for MVP adds infrastructure complexity without proportional benefit."
   181→    },
   182→    {
   183→      "area": "Document Chunking Strategy",
   184→      "chosen": "Recursive character text splitting with markdown-aware boundaries",
   185→      "alternatives_considered": ["Fixed-size chunking", "Semantic chunking"],
   186→      "rationale": "Repository content is primarily markdown (READMEs, docs). Markdown-aware splitting preserves heading structure and avoids splitting mid-section. Recursive character splitting is well-understood and predictable. Semantic chunking adds LLM cost per chunk without clear MVP benefit."
   187→    },
   188→    {
   189→      "area": "Monorepo vs Single App",
   190→      "chosen": "Single Next.js app with src/lib/ module layer",
   191→      "alternatives_considered": ["Turborepo monorepo (as PRD section 6 describes)", "Nx monorepo"],
   192→      "rationale": "The existing codebase is already structured as a single Next.js app with well-organized src/lib/ modules (agents, ai, channels, config, db, github, knowledge, memory, tools, utils). The architecture doc explicitly endorses this pattern. Converting to a monorepo would require significant rework for no V1 benefit. The modular lib layer achieves the same separation of concerns."
   193→    }
   194→  ],
   195→  "questions": [
   196→    {
   197→      "id": "q1",
   198→      "question": "Which vector store should be targeted for production readiness?",
   199→      "options": [
   200→        {
   201→          "value": "pgvector",
   202→          "label": "pgvector (Postgres extension)",
   203→          "description": "Runs inside the existing Postgres database. No additional infrastructure. Good for small-to-medium scale. Simplifies ops but may have lower search performance at scale."
   204→        },
   205→        {
   206→          "value": "pinecone",
   207→          "label": "Pinecone (managed service)",
   208→          "description": "Fully managed, scales automatically. Best search performance. Adds external dependency and cost. Good if expecting rapid knowledge base growth."
   209→        },
   210→        {
   211→          "value": "inmemory",
   212→          "label": "In-memory only (defer decision)",
   213→          "description": "Use in-memory store for V1, defer production vector store choice. Fastest to ship but limits deployment to single-process and loses data on restart."
   214→        }
   215→      ],
   216→      "default": "pgvector"
   217→    },
   218→    {
   219→      "id": "q2",
   220→      "question": "How should repository indexing be triggered and scheduled?",
   221→      "options": [
   222→        {
   223→          "value": "manual",
   224→          "label": "Manual admin trigger only",
   225→          "description": "Admins click a button to index. Simplest implementation. Requires admin attention when repos change."
   226→        },
   227→        {
   228→          "value": "manual_plus_webhook",
   229→          "label": "Manual trigger + GitHub webhook on push",
   230→          "description": "Manual trigger plus automatic re-indexing when repos are updated via GitHub webhooks. More operational complexity but keeps knowledge fresh automatically."
   231→        },
   232→        {
   233→          "value": "manual_plus_cron",
   234→          "label": "Manual trigger + daily scheduled sync",
   235→          "description": "Manual trigger plus a daily cron/scheduled job to check for changes. Middle ground — less real-time than webhooks but doesn't require GitHub webhook setup."
   236→        }
   237→      ],
   238→      "default": "manual"
   239→    },
   240→    {
   241→      "id": "q3",
   242→      "question": "Should the web chat require authentication or allow anonymous usage?",
   243→      "options": [
   244→        {
   245→          "value": "anonymous_first",
   246→          "label": "Anonymous by default, optional sign-in",
   247→          "description": "Anyone can chat without signing in. Firebase Auth is available but not required for V1 public Q&A. Lowest friction for visitors. Admin features still require auth."
   248→        },
   249→        {
   250→          "value": "auth_required",
   251→          "label": "Authentication required for all chat",
   252→          "description": "All users must sign in before chatting. Enables user tracking from day one. Higher friction but simplifies session management and prepares for V2 private data features."
   253→        }
   254→      ],
   255→      "default": "anonymous_first"
   256→    },
   257→    {
   258→      "id": "q4",
   259→      "question": "What level of admin UI should be built for V1?",
   260→      "options": [
   261→        {
   262→          "value": "minimal",
   263→          "label": "Minimal admin page",
   264→          "description": "Single page showing repo list, index trigger buttons, and job status. No auth-gated admin layout. Fastest to build. Admin access controlled by checking role on API endpoints."
   265→        },
   266→        {
   267→          "value": "full_admin",
   268→          "label": "Dedicated admin section with layout",
   269→          "description": "Separate /admin route with its own layout, navigation, and role-gated access. Includes repo management, indexing status dashboard, and audit log viewer. More polished but takes longer."
   270→        }
   271→      ],
   272→      "default": "minimal"
   273→    },
   274→    {
   275→      "id": "q5",
   276→      "question": "Should the LLM response be streamed to the client?",
   277→      "options": [
   278→        {
   279→          "value": "streaming",
   280→          "label": "Streaming responses (SSE/ReadableStream)",
   281→          "description": "Tokens stream to the chat UI as they are generated. Better perceived performance and UX. More complex to implement with tool calls in the agent loop. The existing codebase already has a /api/chat/stream route."
   282→        },
   283→        {
   284→          "value": "non_streaming",
   285→          "label": "Non-streaming (wait for full response)",
   286→          "description": "Client waits for the complete response. Simpler implementation, especially with the ReAct tool-calling loop. Worse perceived latency for longer answers."
   287→        },
   288→        {
   289→          "value": "both",
   290→          "label": "Both (streaming default, non-streaming fallback)",
   291→          "description": "Implement streaming as primary with non-streaming endpoint as fallback. Most flexible but doubles the API surface. The existing codebase has both routes already."
   292→        }

   295→    }
   296→  ]
   297→}

content.content:
1→# Sandra — Implementation PRD (Version 1)
     2→
     3→Organization: EdLight Initiative  
     4→Project: Sandra  
     5→Document Type: Implementation Product Requirements Document  
     6→Status: Active  
     7→Version: 1.0  
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→# 1. Executive Summary
    12→
    13→Sandra is the AI assistant for the EdLight ecosystem.
    14→
    15→Sandra will act as the central conversational interface connecting EdLight platforms such as:
    16→
    17→- EdLight Code
    18→- EdLight Academy
    19→- EdLight News
    20→- EdLight Initiative
    21→
    22→Users should be able to ask Sandra questions about these initiatives and receive responses generated using indexed knowledge from EdLight repositories and documentation.
    23→
    24→This PRD defines the **Version 1 implementation** of Sandra.
    25→
    26→Version 1 focuses on:
    27→
    28→- web chat interface
    29→- knowledge retrieval from EdLight repositories
    30→- structured agent runtime
    31→- tool-based architecture
    32→- secure system foundation
    33→
    34→This version **does not yet include messaging platforms or private user data access**.
    35→
    36→---
    37→
    38→# 2. Implementation Goal
    39→
    40→The goal of Version 1 is to deliver a working Sandra assistant that can:
    41→
    42→- run as a web-based chat interface
    43→- answer questions about EdLight initiatives
    44→- retrieve answers using indexed repository documentation
    45→- maintain conversation sessions
    46→- expose a structured runtime and tool architecture
    47→- provide administrative indexing controls
    48→
    49→Sandra Version 1 should be deployable and usable by end users through the web interface.
    50→
    51→---
    52→
    53→# 3. In Scope
    54→
    55→The following features **must be implemented in Version 1**.
    56→
    57→## Web Chat Interface
    58→Users can open Sandra in a browser and interact through a chat UI.
    59→
    60→## Public Knowledge Question Answering
    61→Sandra can answer questions about:
    62→
    63→- EdLight Code
    64→- EdLight News
    65→- EdLight Academy
    66→- EdLight Initiative
    67→
    68→using indexed repository content.
    69→
    70→## Repository Indexing
    71→Sandra must index the following repositories:
    72→
    73→- https://github.com/edlinitiative/code
    74→- https://github.com/edlinitiative/EdLight-News
    75→- https://github.com/edlinitiative/EdLight-Initiative
    76→- https://github.com/edlinitiative/EdLight-Academy
    77→
    78→README and documentation files must be processed into a retrieval system.
    79→
    80→## Sandra Agent Runtime
    81→Sandra must operate through a centralized runtime that:
    82→
    83→- receives normalized user requests
    84→- retrieves relevant knowledge
    85→- executes tools when necessary
    86→- generates responses
    87→- logs actions
    88→
    89→## Tool-Based Architecture
    90→Sandra must access system functionality through a tool registry rather than direct database access.
    91→
    92→## Admin Indexing Controls
    93→Admins must be able to:
    94→
    95→- list indexed repositories
    96→- trigger indexing manually
    97→- view indexing status
    98→
    99→## Multilingual Foundation
   100→Sandra must support:
   101→
   102→- Haitian Creole
   103→- French
   104→- English
   105→
   106→---
   107→
   108→# 4. Out of Scope
   109→
   110→The following features **must NOT be implemented in Version 1**.
   111→
   112→- WhatsApp integration
   113→- Instagram messaging
   114→- email sending
   115→- voice interaction
   116→- private user data access
   117→- course enrollment retrieval
   118→- certificate retrieval
   119→- autonomous multi-step agent workflows
   120→- advanced analytics dashboards
   121→- cross-platform workflow automation
   122→
   123→These features are planned for later versions.
   124→
   125→---
   126→
   127→# 5. Required Technology Stack
   128→
   129→The implementation must use the following technologies.
   130→
   131→Frontend:
   132→
   133→- Next.js (App Router)
   134→- TypeScript
   135→- Tailwind CSS
   136→
   137→Backend:
   138→
   139→- Next.js server routes
   140→- Node.js runtime
   141→
   142→Authentication:
   143→
   144→- Firebase Auth
   145→
   146→Realtime State:
   147→
   148→- Firestore
   149→
   150→Structured Database:
   151→
   152→- Postgres
   153→- Prisma ORM
   154→
   155→Validation:
   156→
   157→- Zod
   158→
   159→AI Integration:
   160→
   161→- LLM provider abstraction layer
   162→
   163→Knowledge Retrieval:
   164→
   165→- vector store abstraction
   166→
   167→---
   168→
   169→# 6. Required Repository Structure
   170→
   171→The repository must follow this structure.
   172→
   173→apps/
   174→web/
   175→admin/
   176→
   177→services/
   178→agent-runtime/
   179→gateway-api/
   180→indexer/
   181→workers/
   182→
   183→packages/
   184→auth/
   185→channels/
   186→tools/
   187→connectors/
   188→memory/
   189→knowledge/
   190→config/
   191→shared-types/
   192→
   193→docs/
   194→
   195→Explanation:
   196→
   197→apps/web  
   198→Web chat interface.
   199→
   200→apps/admin  
   201→Internal admin UI.
   202→
   203→services/agent-runtime  
   204→Sandra runtime orchestration.
   205→
   206→services/gateway-api  
   207→Internal API exposing tools.
   208→
   209→services/indexer  
   210→Repository indexing pipeline.
   211→
   212→services/workers  
   213→Background jobs.
   214→
   215→packages/auth  
   216→Authentication helpers.
   217→
   218→packages/channels  
   219→Channel adapters.
   220→
   221→packages/tools  
   222→Tool registry and execution.
   223→
   224→packages/connectors  
   225→System integrations.
   226→
   227→packages/memory  
   228→Conversation memory.
   229→
   230→packages/knowledge  
   231→Retrieval logic.
   232→
   233→packages/config  
   234→Environment configuration.
   235→
   236→packages/shared-types  
   237→Shared TypeScript types.
   238→
   239→---
   240→
   241→# 7. Core User Stories
   242→
   243→## User Story 1
   244→
   245→As a visitor,  
   246→I want to ask Sandra questions about EdLight initiatives  
   247→so that I can learn about available programs.
   248→
   249→### Acceptance Criteria
   250→
   251→- user opens Sandra web chat
   252→- user submits question
   253→- Sandra generates answer
   254→- answer uses indexed knowledge when relevant
   255→- conversation history is stored
   256→
   257→---
   258→
   259→## User Story 2
   260→
   261→As a visitor,  
   262→I want Sandra to remember the conversation during the session  
   263→so that I can ask follow-up questions.
   264→
   265→### Acceptance Criteria
   266→
   267→- session ID created for each conversation
   268→- message history stored
   269→- follow-up questions reference prior context
   270→
   271→---
   272→
   273→## User Story 3
   274→
   275→As an administrator,  
   276→I want to trigger repository indexing  
   277→so that Sandra can learn new documentation.
   278→
   279→### Acceptance Criteria
   280→
   281→- admin can list registered repositories
   282→- admin can trigger indexing
   283→- indexing status is visible
   284→
   285→---
   286→
   287→# 8. Data Model (MVP)
   288→
   289→## Postgres Tables
   290→
   291→Required tables:
   292→

# Sandra — Implementation PRD (Version 1)

Organization: EdLight Initiative  
Project: Sandra  
Document Type: Implementation Product Requirements Document  
Status: Active  
Version: 1.0  

---

# 1. Executive Summary

Sandra is the AI assistant for the EdLight ecosystem.

Sandra will act as the central conversational interface connecting EdLight platforms such as:

- EdLight Code
- EdLight Academy
- EdLight News
- EdLight Initiative

Users should be able to ask Sandra questions about these initiatives and receive responses generated using indexed knowledge from EdLight repositories and documentation.

This PRD defines the **Version 1 implementation** of Sandra.

Version 1 focuses on:

- web chat interface
- knowledge retrieval from EdLight repositories
- structured agent runtime
- tool-based architecture
- secure system foundation

This version **does not yet include messaging platforms or private user data access**.

---

# 2. Implementation Goal

The goal of Version 1 is to deliver a working Sandra assistant that can:

- run as a web-based chat interface
- answer questions about EdLight initiatives
- retrieve answers using indexed repository documentation
- maintain conversation sessions
- expose a structured runtime and tool architecture
- provide administrative indexing controls

Sandra Version 1 should be deployable and usable by end users through the web interface.

---

# 3. In Scope

The following features **must be implemented in Version 1**.

## Web Chat Interface
Users can open Sandra in a browser and interact through a chat UI.

## Public Knowledge Question Answering
Sandra can answer questions about:

- EdLight Code
- EdLight News
- EdLight Academy
- EdLight Initiative

using indexed repository content.

## Repository Indexing
Sandra must index the following repositories:

- https://github.com/edlinitiative/code
- https://github.com/edlinitiative/EdLight-News
- https://github.com/edlinitiative/EdLight-Initiative
- https://github.com/edlinitiative/EdLight-Academy

README and documentation files must be processed into a retrieval system.

## Sandra Agent Runtime
Sandra must operate through a centralized runtime that:

- receives normalized user requests
- retrieves relevant knowledge
- executes tools when necessary
- generates responses
- logs actions

## Tool-Based Architecture
Sandra must access system functionality through a tool registry rather than direct database access.

## Admin Indexing Controls
Admins must be able to:

- list indexed repositories
- trigger indexing manually
- view indexing status

## Multilingual Foundation
Sandra must support:

- Haitian Creole
- French
- English

---

# 4. Out of Scope

The following features **must NOT be implemented in Version 1**.

- WhatsApp integration
- Instagram messaging
- email sending
- voice interaction
- private user data access
- course enrollment retrieval
- certificate retrieval
- autonomous multi-step agent workflows
- advanced analytics dashboards
- cross-platform workflow automation

These features are planned for later versions.

---

# 5. Required Technology Stack

The implementation must use the following technologies.

Frontend:

- Next.js (App Router)
- TypeScript
- Tailwind CSS

Backend:

- Next.js server routes
- Node.js runtime

Authentication:

- Firebase Auth

Realtime State:

- Firestore

Structured Database:

- Postgres
- Prisma ORM

Validation:

- Zod

AI Integration:

- LLM provider abstraction layer

Knowledge Retrieval:

- vector store abstraction

---

# 6. Required Repository Structure

The repository must follow this structure.

apps/
web/
admin/

services/
agent-runtime/
gateway-api/
indexer/
workers/

packages/
auth/
channels/
tools/
connectors/
memory/
knowledge/
config/
shared-types/

docs/

Explanation:

apps/web  
Web chat interface.

apps/admin  
Internal admin UI.

services/agent-runtime  
Sandra runtime orchestration.

services/gateway-api  
Internal API exposing tools.

services/indexer  
Repository indexing pipeline.

services/workers  
Background jobs.

packages/auth  
Authentication helpers.

packages/channels  
Channel adapters.

packages/tools  
Tool registry and execution.

packages/connectors  
System integrations.

packages/memory  
Conversation memory.

packages/knowledge  
Retrieval logic.

packages/config  
Environment configuration.

packages/shared-types  
Shared TypeScript types.

---

# 7. Core User Stories

## User Story 1

As a visitor,  
I want to ask Sandra questions about EdLight initiatives  
so that I can learn about available programs.

### Acceptance Criteria

- user opens Sandra web chat
- user submits question
- Sandra generates answer
- answer uses indexed knowledge when relevant
- conversation history is stored

---

## User Story 2

As a visitor,  
I want Sandra to remember the conversation during the session  
so that I can ask follow-up questions.

### Acceptance Criteria

- session ID created for each conversation
- message history stored
- follow-up questions reference prior context

---

## User Story 3

As an administrator,  
I want to trigger repository indexing  
so that Sandra can learn new documentation.

### Acceptance Criteria

- admin can list registered repositories
- admin can trigger indexing
- indexing status is visible

---

# 8. Data Model (MVP)

## Postgres Tables

Required tables:

users  
initiatives  
repo_registry  
indexed_sources  
indexed_documents  
index_jobs  
tool_executions  
audit_logs  

---

## Firestore Collections

chat_sessions  

Subcollection:

chat_sessions/{sessionId}/messages

---

# 9. Tool Contracts (MVP)

Sandra must implement the following tools.

## searchKnowledgeBase

Description  
Search indexed knowledge.

Required scope  
read:public_content

Input

{
“query”: “What is EdLight Code?”
}

Output

{
“results”: []
}

---

## getEdLightInitiatives

Description  
Return list of initiatives.

Required scope  
read:public_content

Input

{}

Output

{
“initiatives”: []
}

---

## lookupRepoInfo

Description  
Return repository metadata.

Required scope  
read:public_content

Input

{
“repoFullName”: “edlinitiative/code”
}

Output

{
“name”: “”,
“repoUrl”: “”
}

---

## triggerRepoIndexing

Description  
Trigger indexing job.

Required scope  
admin:indexing

Input

{
“repoId”: “”
}

Output

{
“jobId”: “”
}

---

## getIndexingStatus

Description  
Return indexing job status.

Required scope  
admin:indexing

Input

{
“jobId”: “”
}

Output

{
“status”: “”
}

---

# 10. API Contracts

## POST /api/sandra/chat

Main chat endpoint.

Input

{
“sessionId”: “”,
“message”: “”,
“language”: “en”
}

Output

{
“reply”: “”,
“sessionId”: “”,
“messageId”: “”
}

---

## GET /api/sandra/conversations/:sessionId

Returns session messages.

---

## GET /api/sandra/repos

Lists registered repositories.

---

## POST /api/sandra/repos/:repoId/index

Triggers indexing job.

---

## GET /api/sandra/index-jobs/:jobId

Returns job status.

---

## GET /api/sandra/health

Health check endpoint.

---

# 11. Repository Indexing Requirements

The indexing system must:

1. fetch repository README files
2. fetch documentation files
3. extract text
4. chunk documents
5. generate embeddings
6. store vector references
7. track metadata in Postgres

Indexed content must be retrievable by the Sandra runtime.

---

# 12. Security Constraints

The implementation must enforce the following rules.

- Sandra runtime must not query raw databases directly
- all database access must go through services
- tool execution must enforce permission scopes
- tool calls must be logged
- admin actions must be logged
- environment secrets must never be exposed to client code

---

# 13. Implementation Milestones

The system must be implemented in this order.

1. repository structure and config
2. shared types and environment validation
3. Firebase Auth integration
4. Prisma/Postgres schema
5. Firestore chat persistence
6. Sandra runtime
7. tool registry
8. GitHub indexing pipeline
9. retrieval system
10. API routes
11. web chat UI
12. admin indexing interface
13. documentation

---

# 14. Deliverables

The following components must exist after implementation.

- working web chat assistant
- repository indexing pipeline
- retrieval-based responses
- tool registry system
- admin indexing controls
- Firebase authentication
- Firestore session storage
- Postgres database schema
- documented architecture

---

# 15. Future Versions

Future versions will include:

- authenticated user data tools
- WhatsApp integration
- Instagram integration
- email assistant
- voice interaction
- personalized learning guidance
- agent workflows

These features are intentionally excluded from Version 1.

---

# 16. Success Criteria

Sandra Version 1 will be considered successful when:

- users can chat with Sandra through the web interface
- Sandra answers questions about EdLight initiatives
- responses use indexed repository knowledge
- conversations persist during sessions
- admins can manage repository indexing
- the architecture supports future expansion

1→# Sandra — Architecture
     2→
     3→## System Overview
     4→
     5→Sandra is built as a Next.js application with a modular library layer that contains all business logic. The architecture follows clean separation of concerns: UI components never contain business logic, and the library layer is framework-agnostic where possible.
     6→
     7→```
     8→┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
     9→│              Next.js App Layer                │
    10→│  ┌────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐ │
    11→│  │ Pages  │  │ API      │  │ Server       │ │
    12→│  │ (React)│  │ Routes   │  │ Actions      │ │
    13→│  └───┬────┘  └────┬─────┘  └──────┬───────┘ │
    14→│      │            │               │          │
    15→│      └────────────┼───────────────┘          │
    16→│                   │                          │
    17→│   ┌───────────────▼──────────────────────┐   │
    18→│   │         Library Layer (src/lib/)     │   │
    19→│   │                                      │   │
    20→│   │  ┌─────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐   │   │
    21→│   │  │ Agents  │  │  Knowledge/RAG   │   │   │
    22→│   │  │         │  │  - Chunker       │   │   │
    23→│   │  │ - Sandra│  │  - Embeddings    │   │   │
    24→│   │  │ - Prompt│  │  - Vector Store  │   │   │
    25→│   │  │ - Tools │  │  - Retrieval     │   │   │
    26→│   │  └─────────┘  └──────────────────┘   │   │
    27→│   │                                      │   │
    28→│   │  ┌─────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐   │   │
    29→│   │  │   AI    │  │    Channels      │   │   │
    30→│   │  │Provider │  │  - Web           │   │   │
    31→│   │  │- OpenAI │  │  - WhatsApp      │   │   │
    32→│   │  │- (...)  │  │  - Instagram     │   │   │
    33→│   │  └─────────┘  │  - Email/Voice   │   │   │
    34→│   │               └──────────────────┘   │   │
    35→│   │                                      │   │
    36→│   │  ┌─────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐   │   │
    37→│   │  │ Memory  │  │    GitHub        │   │   │
    38→│   │  │- Session│  │  - Client        │   │   │
    39→│   │  │- User   │  │  - Fetcher       │   │   │
    40→│   │  └─────────┘  │  - Indexer       │   │   │
    41→│   │               └──────────────────┘   │   │
    42→│   │                                      │   │
    43→│   │  ┌─────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐   │   │
    44→│   │  │ Config  │  │    Utils         │   │   │
    45→│   │  │ - Env   │  │  - Errors        │   │   │
    46→│   │  │ - Const │  │  - Logger        │   │   │
    47→│   │  └─────────┘  │  - Validation    │   │   │
    48→│   │               └──────────────────┘   │   │
    49→│   └──────────────────────────────────────┘   │
    50→│                                              │
    51→│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐   │
    52→│   │            Data Layer                │   │
    53→│   │  Prisma + PostgreSQL  │  Vector Store│   │
    54→│   └──────────────────────────────────────┘   │
    55→└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    56→```
    57→
    58→## Key Design Decisions
    59→
    60→### 1. Provider Abstraction
    61→All AI functionality goes through the `AIProvider` interface. The OpenAI implementation is the first, but Anthropic and Google can be added by implementing the same interface. No code outside `src/lib/ai/` knows which provider is being used.
    62→
    63→### 2. Agent Loop Pattern
    64→Sandra uses a **ReAct-style agent loop**:
    65→1. Receive input
    66→2. Build context (memory, retrieval, tools)
    67→3. Call LLM
    68→4. If tool calls → execute → loop back to step 3
    69→5. Return final response
    70→
    71→This pattern is proven at scale and allows Sandra to evolve from a simple Q&A bot to a full agent that takes actions.
    72→
    73→### 3. Memory Architecture
    74→Two tiers:
    75→- **Session memory** — Short-term conversation context. Lives in memory or Redis.
    76→- **User memory** — Long-term facts about users. Persisted in PostgreSQL.
    77→
    78→Both are behind interfaces so the storage backend can be swapped without changing the agent.
    79→
    80→### 4. Knowledge/RAG Pipeline
    81→Documents flow through: **Fetch → Chunk → Embed → Store → Retrieve**
    82→
    83→Each step is a separate function/class, making it easy to:
    84→- Change chunking strategies
    85→- Swap embedding models
    86→- Replace the vector store
    87→- Add new document sources
    88→
    89→### 5. Channel Normalization
    90→Every channel adapter converts platform-specific payloads into a standard `InboundMessage` format. This means the agent doesn't need to know whether a message came from WhatsApp or the web.
    91→
    92→### 6. Tool Registry
    93→Tools are self-registering: they import the registry and add themselves. The agent reads from the registry at runtime. Adding a new tool is: create file → implement interface → import in index.ts.
    94→
    95→## Data Flow: Chat Message
    96→
    97→```
    98→User types message in web UI
    99→        │
   100→        ▼
   101→POST /api/chat { message, sessionId, language }
   102→        │
   103→        ▼
   104→Zod validation
   105→        │
   106→        ▼
   107→runSandraAgent(input)
   108→        │
   109→        ├── Load session memory (conversation history)
   110→        ├── Load user memory (long-term facts)
   111→        ├── Retrieve relevant knowledge (vector search)
   112→        ├── Build system prompt
   113→        │
   114→        ▼
   115→AI Provider: chatCompletion(messages + tools)
   116→        │
   117→        ├── If tool_calls: execute tools → loop
   118→        │
   119→        ▼
   120→Return response
   121→        │
   122→        ├── Save to session memory
   123→        │
   124→        ▼
   125→JSON response to client
   126→```
   127→
   128→## Configuration
   129→
   130→All configuration is environment-variable driven with Zod validation at startup. The `env.ts` module fails fast in production if required variables are missing, and uses safe defaults in development.
   131→
   132→## Error Handling
   133→
   134→All errors flow through `SandraError` subclasses. API routes catch errors and return structured JSON responses with error codes. The logger provides structured output for debugging.
   135→
   136→## Scalability Path
   137→
   138→1. **Vector Store**: Replace `InMemoryVectorStore` with Pinecone/Qdrant/pgvector
   139→2. **Memory**: Replace in-memory stores with Redis (session) and PostgreSQL (user)
   140→3. **Indexing**: Add a job queue (BullMQ) for background indexing
   141→4. **Channels**: Add webhook endpoints for WhatsApp/Instagram
   142→5. **Auth**: Add NextAuth.js for user authentication
   143→6. **Monitoring**: Add OpenTelemetry for observability
   144→
   145→
   146→# Sandra — Full System Architecture
   147→
   148→Organization: EdLight Initiative  
   149→Project: Sandra  
   150→Document Type: System Architecture  
   151→Status: Draft  
   152→Version: 1.0  
   153→
   154→---
   155→
   156→# 1. Purpose
   157→
   158→Sandra is the central AI assistant and future agent layer for the EdLight ecosystem.
   159→
   160→She is designed to become the unified conversational interface across EdLight platforms, allowing users to interact with EdLight services through natural language instead of navigating multiple websites or systems manually.
   161→
   162→Sandra should support:
   163→
   164→- Web chat
   165→- WhatsApp
   166→- Instagram
   167→- Email
   168→- Live voice interactions
   169→- Haitian Creole, French, and English
   170→- Knowledge retrieval across EdLight repositories and documentation
   171→- Secure access to user-specific data when authorized
   172→- Tool-based actions across EdLight systems
   173→
   174→This architecture is designed to support both:
   175→
   176→- a fast MVP
   177→- a scalable long-term platform
   178→
   179→---
   180→
   181→# 2. High-Level Architecture
   182→
   183→Sandra should be built as a layered system.
   184→
   185→## Core Layers
   186→
   187→1. Channel Layer  
   188→2. Identity and Access Layer  
   189→3. Agent Runtime Layer  
   190→4. Tool and Gateway Layer  
   191→5. Knowledge and Retrieval Layer  
   192→6. Data Layer  
   193→7. Integration Layer  
   194→8. Worker and Automation Layer  
   195→9. Admin and Observability Layer  
   196→
   197→The central architectural principle is:
   198→
   199→**All channels feed into one Sandra runtime, and Sandra interacts with systems only through controlled tools and internal services.**
   200→
   201→Sandra must not be designed as a collection of disconnected bots.  
   202→She should be one core system with multiple interfaces.
   203→
   204→---
   205→
   206→# 3. Architectural Principles
   207→
   208→## 3.1 One Brain, Many Channels
   209→
   210→Sandra should have one shared agent runtime and one shared tool layer.
   211→
   212→Web chat, WhatsApp, Instagram, email, and voice should all send normalized requests into the same core system.
   213→
   214→This ensures:
   215→
   216→- consistent behavior
   217→- shared memory
   218→- easier maintenance
   219→- easier rollout of new capabilities
   220→
   221→---
   222→
   223→## 3.2 Tool-Based System Access
   224→
   225→Sandra should never be given unrestricted access to raw databases or random services.
   226→
   227→Sandra should interact with systems through structured tools such as:
   228→
   229→- `getUserCertificates`
   230→- `getEnrollmentStatus`
   231→- `getLatestNews`
   232→- `searchKnowledgeBase`
   233→- `getProgramDetails`
   234→- `sendWhatsappReply`
   235→- `draftEmail`
   236→
   237→This keeps the system secure, auditable, and easier to evolve.
   238→
   239→---
   240→
   241→## 3.3 Public Knowledge and Private Data Must Be Separate
   242→
   243→Sandra should support two different access modes:
   244→
   245→### Public or system-level mode
   246→Used for:
   247→
   248→- FAQs
   249→- public course information
   250→- public program details
   251→- public news summaries
   252→- public EdLight documentation
   253→
   254→### User-authorized mode
   255→Used for:
   256→
   257→- user certificates
   258→- user enrollments
   259→- application status
   260→- user preferences
   261→- private messages
   262→- personalized guidance
   263→
   264→Private data should only be accessed when the user is authenticated and authorized.
   265→
   266→---
   267→
   268→## 3.4 Fast MVP, Strong Long-Term Foundation
   269→
   270→The MVP should be easy to ship.  
   271→The long-term design should not force a rewrite.
   272→
   273→That means:
   274→
   275→- modular connectors
   276→- modular tools
   277→- a clean gateway layer
   278→- a clear split between realtime app data and structured business data
   279→
   280→---

   341→  # Sandra — Data Model and API/Tool Contract Specification
   342→
   343→Organization: EdLight Initiative  
   344→Project: Sandra  
   345→Document Type: Data Model and Interface Specification  
   346→Status: Draft  
   347→Version: 1.0  
   348→
   349→---
   350→
   351→# 1. Purpose
   352→
   353→This document defines the core data model for Sandra and the contracts between:
   354→
   355→- Sandra Runtime
   356→- Sandra Gateway API
   357→- internal tools
   358→- connectors
   359→- EdLight systems
   360→- channel adapters
   361→
   362→It is designed to complement the main architecture document.
   363→
   364→This specification covers:
   365→
   366→- Postgres data model
   367→- Firestore data model
   368→- vector knowledge model
   369→- tool contract design
   370→- API contract design
   371→- permission model
   372→- audit model
   373→
   374→The goal is to ensure Sandra can scale from a simple assistant into a secure multi-system AI platform for the EdLight ecosystem.
   375→
   376→---
   377→
   378→# 2. Core Design Principles
   379→
   380→## 2.1 Source-of-Truth Separation
   381→
   382→Sandra should not store all data in one place.
   383→
   384→Different categories of data should live in the storage layer best suited for them:
   385→
   386→- Firestore for realtime conversation and client-facing interaction state
   387→- Postgres for structured relational business and operational data
   388→- Vector store for unstructured semantic knowledge retrieval
   389→
   390→---
   391→
   392→## 2.2 Tool-Mediated Data Access
   393→
   394→Sandra Runtime must not directly access raw tables or collections.
   395→
   396→All sensitive and structured data access should happen through:
   397→
   398→- internal APIs
   399→- tool contracts
   400→- connectors
   401→
   402→This ensures:
   403→
   404→- cleaner architecture
   405→- strict permission enforcement
   406→- observability
   407→- easier future refactoring
   408→
   409→---
   410→
   411→## 2.3 Public and Private Data Separation
   412→
   413→Sandra must clearly separate:
   414→
   415→### Public data
   416→Examples:
   417→
   418→- public initiative descriptions
   419→- public course catalog
   420→- public FAQs
   421→- repo documentation
   422→- public news content
   423→
   424→### Private data
   425→Examples:
   426→
   427→- user enrollments
   428→- certificates
   429→- application status
   430→- contact details
   431→- consent records
   432→- channel connection metadata
   433→
   434→Private data should only be accessible with valid identity and permission checks.
   435→
   436→---
   437→
   438→# 3. Canonical Identity Model
   439→
   440→Sandra should maintain a canonical user model in Postgres, while Firebase Auth acts as the main authentication provider.
   441→
   442→## Identity Components
   443→
   444→### Firebase Auth
   445→Used for:
   446→
   447→- sign-in
   448→- token issuance
   449→- session authentication
   450→- role claims for app clients
   451→
   452→### Postgres Canonical User
   453→Used for:
   454→
   455→- persistent user record
   456→- cross-system identity mapping
   457→- platform relationships
   458→- internal permissions
   459→- operational logic
   460→
   461→## Identity Flow
   462→
   463→1. user authenticates through Firebase Auth  
   464→2. backend validates Firebase token  
   465→3. backend maps Firebase UID to canonical user in Postgres  
   466→4. Sandra Runtime uses canonical user ID internally  
   467→
   468→This allows Sandra to keep one internal identity even if users interact across multiple EdLight systems.
   469→
   470→---
   471→
   472→# 4. Postgres Data Model

   486→- `full_name` text nullable
   487→- `preferred_language` text nullable
   488→- `country` text nullable
   489→- `timezone` text nullable

   496→This is the main record used across Sandra tools and connectors.

  1013→- `email`
  1014→- `voice`
